SFLWR3: Chapter 6 (answer key)

Stefan Th. Gries (UC Santa Barbara & JLU Giessen)

2021-01-01 12:34:56

  • 1 Hasselgård & Johansson (2011): quite in NS & NNS English
    • 1.1 Critique & considering improvements
    • 1.2 Analysis 1: all the data
      • 1.2.1 Model fitting/selection
      • 1.2.2 Model interpretation
    • 1.3 Interlude
    • 1.4 Analysis 2: learner files with at least 1 quite and native speakers
      • 1.4.1 Model fitting/selection
      • 1.4.2 Model interpretation
    • 1.5 Concluding remarks
  • 2 Parviainen & Fuchs (2018)
    • 2.1 Replicating their analyses
      • 2.1.1 Modeling also
      • 2.1.2 Modeling only
    • 2.2 Critique & considering improvements
      • 2.2.1 Improvement 1: the ‘big’ model
        • 2.2.1.1 The effect of GENDER
        • 2.2.1.2 The effect of AGE
        • 2.2.1.3 The effect of VARIETY:PARTICLE
      • 2.2.2 Improvement 2: the ‘big’ model with weights
    • 2.3 Concluding remarks
  • 3 Optional “that”-complementation in native and learner English

1 Hasselgård & Johansson (2011): quite in NS & NNS English

(This exercise is based on Gries 2018.) This learner corpus (case) study looked, among other things, at the frequencies with with native speakers of English and learners of English used quite in writing. I wrote an R script to search the relevant corpora and approximate their data as much as possible; the result of that corpus retrieval process is in the file <_inputfiles/06_quite-ex.rds>. This file is an .rds file, a compressed R-specific file; this is because it has too many rows for most spreadsheet software to work with anyway (>1m) and because its highly repetitive content makes it a file that benefits particularly much from compression.

rm(list=ls(all.names=TRUE))
summary(x <- readRDS("../_inputfiles/06_quite-ex.rds"))
##       CASE         QUITE          L1                FILE        
##  Min.   :      1   no :1197086   EN:323898   BRSUR1_01:   3704  
##  1st Qu.: 299385   yes:    451   FR:227361   BRSUR1_05:   3545  
##  Median : 598769                 GE:232773   SPAL1005 :   3395  
##  Mean   : 598769                 NO:213716   BRSUR1_15:   3161  
##  3rd Qu.: 898153                 SP:199789   BRSUR1_04:   3071  
##  Max.   :1197537                             BRSUR1_09:   2995  
##                                              (Other)  :1177666

To state the maybe obvious, the languages in the L1 column are English (i.e. the native speakers), French, German, Norwegian, and Spanish.

addmargins(quite.by.l1 <- table(x$QUITE, x$L1))  # observed frequencies
##      
##            EN      FR      GE      NO      SP     Sum
##   no   323831  227279  232627  213624  199725 1197086
##   yes      67      82     146      92      64     451
##   Sum  323898  227361  232773  213716  199789 1197537
quite.by.l1[2,] / colSums(quite.by.l1) * 100000  #  quite per 100K words
##       EN       FR       GE       NO       SP 
## 20.68552 36.06599 62.72205 43.04778 32.03380

The match of these normalized frequencies with the data reported in the paper we’re discussing here is not perfect, but good enough for our present purposes; these are the frequencies/100K words they reported (in their Table 2), which are correlated with mine with a Pearson’s r of 0.998:

LOCNESS ICLE-FR ICLE-GE ICLE-NO ICLE-SP
20.5 38 62.3 43.7 31.8

The authors then report the results of some chi-squared tests that somehow contrast each learner group with the native speakers. Specifically, this is what they say:

Table 2 [see above] shows that quite is overused in all the learner groups but most markedly so among the Germans, followed at a distance by the Norwegians (both at significance levels of p<0.01).[footnote: The ICLE frequencies were found using the statistics function on the ICLEv2 CD, while LOCNESS was analysed using the corpus tool AntConc […]. The frequencies from each ICLE sub-corpus and LOCNESS were compared using chi-square.] The overuse of quite in ICLE-GE ties in with the general overuse of adjective modification by German learners identified by Lorenz (1998: 57). In ICLE-FR and ICLE-SP, the overuse is less dramatic (significant at p<0.05 for ICLE-FR, but less obviously so at p=0.1 for ICLE-SP). The overall frequency distribution shown in Table 2 thus seems to reflect the Germanic – Romance distinction.

Your task is to (i) pretend you’re a reviewer tasked with reviewing this case study to decide whether it should be published or not and (ii) analyze these data in a better way.

1.1 Critique & considering improvements

Let’s begin with a very general observation: he authors then claim that the “overall frequency distribution […] seems to reflect the Germanic-Romance distinction.” Frankly, I don’t see it. The value for the Norwegian learners is much closer to the French and the Spanish values than it is to the German value – on what empirical ground(s) does NO get grouped with GE and not with FR or even FR and SP? We don’t get that information.

This leads to the first of 3 more pressing problems. The problem one is simply the incompleteness of the reporting. We already saw a first example of that in the previous paragraph, but there’s worse. For instance, we don’t even get to know whether they computed chi-squared tests for goodness-of-fit (e.g., every learner L1 separately against the native speakers) like this:

# are these chi-squared tests for goodness-of-fit the ones they did?
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,2], p=quite.by.l1[,1], rescale.p=TRUE)$p.value # FR vs. EN
## [1] 3.403073e-07
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,3], p=quite.by.l1[,1], rescale.p=TRUE)$p.value # GE vs. EN
## [1] 3.650904e-45
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,4], p=quite.by.l1[,1], rescale.p=TRUE)$p.value # NO vs. EN
## [1] 6.544698e-13
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,5], p=quite.by.l1[,1], rescale.p=TRUE)$p.value # SP vs. EN
## [1] 0.0004200173

… or whether they computed chi-squared tests for independence (on multiple versions of quite.by.l1) like this:

# are these chi-squared tests for independence the ones they did?
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,c(1,2)], correct=FALSE)$p.value # EN vs. FR
## [1] 0.0006267351
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,c(1,3)], correct=FALSE)$p.value # EN vs. GE
## [1] 2.568593e-15
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,c(1,4)], correct=FALSE)$p.value # EN vs. NO
## [1] 3.061701e-06
chisq.test(quite.by.l1[,c(1,5)], correct=FALSE)$p.value # EN vs. SP
## [1] 0.01165102

And we can’t even figure that out by ourselves because we don’t get exact p-values that we could compare to our own (also no dfs, no effect sizes, nothing).

The second problem is that the authors state no hypotheses, which means that they did 4 chi-squared tests on the same data set, but multiple chi-squared tests of any kind like the above that do not test a priori hypotheses would usually have to be corrected for multiple post-hoc testing, which the authors didn’t do. Since the authors are reporting p-values of 0.1 (for ICLE-SP), <0.05 (for ICLE-FR), and <0.01 for ICLE-NO and ICLE-GE, it’s clear that a correction for multiple testing would have made at least some of the results currently described as significant not significant.

But the biggest problems is the third. While it is unclear how Hasselgård & Johansson actually did their chi-squared tests, one thing, however, is crystal-clear: They shouldn’t even have done any chi-squared tests to begin with because, as you know from Chapter 4, that test presupposes that the data points are independent, which they are not: Each speaker contributed many data points, namely as many data points as there are words in their essay. Thus, as weird and as overkill it may seem, this data set is actually a candidate for mixed-effects model kind of analysis.

To do that, let’s first define some useful contrasts as we did in Section 5.2.3.3:

x$L1contr <- x$L1
contrasts(x$L1contr) <- cbind(
#                         en   fren   germ  norw  span
   native_vs_nonnative= c(0.8, -0.2,  -0.2, -0.2, -0.2), # contrast 1
   romance_vs_germanic= c(0  ,  0.5,  -0.5, -0.5,  0.5), # contrast 2
   spanish_vs_french=   c(0  , -0.5,   0  ,  0  ,  0.5), # contrast 3
   norwergian_vs_german=c(0  ,  0  ,  -0.5,  0.5,  0  )) # contrast 4
summary(x <- x[,c(1:3,5,4)])
##       CASE         QUITE          L1         L1contr            FILE        
##  Min.   :      1   no :1197086   EN:323898   EN:323898   BRSUR1_01:   3704  
##  1st Qu.: 299385   yes:    451   FR:227361   FR:227361   BRSUR1_05:   3545  
##  Median : 598769                 GE:232773   GE:232773   SPAL1005 :   3395  
##  Mean   : 598769                 NO:213716   NO:213716   BRSUR1_15:   3161  
##  3rd Qu.: 898153                 SP:199789   SP:199789   BRSUR1_04:   3071  
##  Max.   :1197537                                         BRSUR1_09:   2995  
##                                                          (Other)  :1177666

1.2 Analysis 1: all the data

1.2.1 Model fitting/selection

Ok, let’s run the model:

library(effects); library(emmeans); library(lme4); library(MuMIn)
summary(m.01.all <- glmer(
   QUITE ~ 1 + # QUITE ~ an overall intercept (1)
   L1contr +   # L1contr as a predictor
   (1|FILE),   # w/ varying intercepts for each file
   family=binomial, data=x), correlation=FALSE)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace Approximation) ['glmerMod']
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: QUITE ~ 1 + L1contr + (1 | FILE)
##    Data: x
## 
##       AIC       BIC    logLik  deviance  df.resid 
##    7811.7    7883.7   -3899.9    7799.7   1197531 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -0.090 -0.015 -0.012 -0.010 70.262 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  FILE   (Intercept) 1.819    1.349   
## Number of obs: 1197537, groups:  FILE, 1764
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)                  -8.7274     0.1139 -76.647  < 2e-16 ***
## L1contrnative_vs_nonnative   -0.6513     0.1649  -3.949 7.85e-05 ***
## L1contrromance_vs_germanic   -0.3940     0.1444  -2.729  0.00636 ** 
## L1contrspanish_vs_french     -0.1005     0.2212  -0.454  0.64962    
## L1contrnorwergian_vs_german  -0.2807     0.1858  -1.511  0.13075    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
saveRDS(m.01.all, file="../_outputfiles/06_exercises_answerkey_m.01.all.RDS")
drop1(           # drop each droppable predictor at a time
   m.01.all,     # from the model m.01.all &
   test="Chisq") # test its significance w/ a LR-test
## Single term deletions
## 
## Model:
## QUITE ~ 1 + L1contr + (1 | FILE)
##         npar    AIC    LRT   Pr(Chi)    
## <none>       7811.7                     
## L1contr    4 7831.6 27.847 1.339e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

So, the model is highly significant – not very surprising given that we have ≈1.12m data points. How well does the model do, though?

C.score(m.01.all)
## [1] 0.926173
r.squaredGLMM(m.01.all)
##                      R2m          R2c
## theoretical 2.350129e-02 0.3711639776
## delta       5.214492e-05 0.0008235427

The C-score of 0.926 is very good; but the R2 are terrible: R2marginal, the value for the fixef of L1, is a pitiful 0.024; R2conditional is much better (0.371), but that just means there’s a lot of variability between speakers/files (which, by definition, the above authors’ chi-squared approach missed, but which one might also not be that interested in that much, unless the study involved individual-variation questions).

1.2.2 Model interpretation

Let’s check the classification accuracy of this model:

x$PREDS.NUM <- predict( # make x$PREDS.NUM the result of predicting
   m.01.all,            # from m.01.all
   type="response")     # predicted probabilities of "yes"
x$PREDS.CAT <- factor(      # make x$PREDS.CAT the result of checking
   ifelse(x$PREDS.NUM>=0.5, # if the predicted prob of "yes" is >=0.5
      levels(x$QUITE)[2],   # if yes, predict "yes"
      levels(x$QUITE)[1]))  # otherwise, predict "no"
(qwe <- table(x$QUITE, x$PREDS.CAT)) # confusion matrix & its eval
##      
##            no
##   no  1197086
##   yes     451

Of course … the model never predicts quite, it has no discriminatory power at all.

Let’s visualize. We try the normal kind of effects plot first but, given the current data/results, of course not with the complete 0-to-1 range of the y axis that we usually do:

plot(allEffects(m.01.all), type="response", ylim=c(0, 0.0005), grid=TRUE)

And then let’s compute all pairwise comparisons. However, these are not our a priori contrasts, which means now we’re kinda fishing and so we do correct for multiple post-hoc tests.

pairs( # compute pairwise comparisons
    emmeans(            # of means computed
      object=m.01.all,  # for the model m.01.all
       ~ L1contr),      # namely all contrasts of this predictor
   adjust="Holm")       # adjust for all comparisons
##  contrast estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  EN - FR    -0.505 0.207 Inf  -2.432  0.0901
##  EN - GE    -0.989 0.191 Inf  -5.165  <.0001
##  EN - NO    -0.708 0.205 Inf  -3.446  0.0051
##  EN - SP    -0.404 0.222 Inf  -1.816  0.3466
##  FR - GE    -0.484 0.189 Inf  -2.567  0.0718
##  FR - NO    -0.203 0.203 Inf  -1.002  0.6322
##  FR - SP     0.100 0.221 Inf   0.454  0.6496
##  GE - NO     0.281 0.186 Inf   1.511  0.5230
##  GE - SP     0.585 0.205 Inf   2.845  0.0356
##  NO - SP     0.304 0.219 Inf   1.389  0.5230
## 
## Results are given on the log odds ratio (not the response) scale. 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 10 tests

So, bottom line: The overall model is significant (LR-test=27.85, df=4, p<0.0001) and a nice C-score (≈0.921) but a terrible R2marginal (≈0.024) zero discriminatory power (because it’s recall is 0: it never predicts quite). But when it comes to interpreting these data, things become more ‘interesting’ because this is one of these cases where it really matters a lot what your hypotheses were. Crucially, the original authors of this case study specified no hypotheses so would theoretically only be allowed to pursue the emmeans approach. Thus, if they hadn’t done the wrong chi-squared kind of analyses but the right kind of mixed-effects model, their conclusions would have been the above-mentioned overall model statistics and then that the exhaustive comparisons with emmeans show that, with the required control for multiple tests, the results are that

  • the native speakers use quite significantly less often than the Norwegian and German learners, but not than the Spanish or French learners;
  • the only other significant difference is that the German learners use quite significantly more often than the Spanish learners.

In other words, given the absence of stated hypotheses, the actual results support neither their postulated overuse of all the learner groups nor the Germanic vs. Romance distinction.

If, on the other hand, the authors had stipulated and motivated a priori(!) hypotheses that (i) learners overuse quite and that (ii) there is a Germanic vs. Romance distinction, then they could have gone with the results from the summary table with the a priori orthogonal contrasts, which would then have worked out as hypothesized:

  • yes, all non-native speakers use quite more often than all native speakers, but
  • the difference between the two L1 families of romance vs. germanic is significant;
  • the contrasts within the two L1 families are not significant.

Either way, since this analysis corrects for the repeated measurements for the speakers, these results are more authoritative than the series of unspecified chi-squared tests, regardless of what the hypotheses were. But … the fact remains that the model is generally really crappy: recall the terrible R2, accuracy and precision at baseline, recall at 0, … What might be behind this? Two reasons come to mind – the first of this is touched upon in the following interlude.

1.3 Interlude

The previous analysis glossed over something: Many files might not contain the word quite – (i) how many files don’t? and (ii) do we want to treat those all alike? As for (i), this touches upon the notion of dispersion. A proportion of uses of quite is essentially a mean (of scoring QUITE: yes as 1 and QUITE: no as 0) and I told you (and everyone else) to never report a measure of central tendency without (also considering) a measure of dispersion. The mixed-effects model of course could correct for that because it could boost the predicted probability of quite for each file that contained at least 1 quite with a positive intercept adjustment – and that’s what it did:

# the frequencies of quite per file
head(quite.by.file <- table(x$FILE, x$QUITE))
##         
##           no yes
##   Beef01 619   0
##   Beef02 432   1
##   Beef03 526   0
##   Beef04 735   0
##   Beef05 368   0
##   Beef06 356   0
# the varying intercept adjustments per file
head(ranef(m.01.all)[[1]])
##        (Intercept)
## Beef01 -0.09822964
## Beef02  1.48447099
## Beef03 -0.08461545
## Beef04 -0.11472925
## Beef05 -0.06063527
## Beef06 -0.05876767
table(quite.by.file[,2]>0,         # cross-tabulate whether a file had quite w/
   sign(ranef(m.01.all)[[1]][,1])) # the sign of the intercept adjustment
##        
##           -1    1
##   FALSE 1443    0
##   TRUE     0  321

But now look at this: the vast majority of files in the data do not even contain quite: 1443/(1443+321)≈0.818, i.e. nearly 82% of the files don’t have the word in them at all. The following plot shows for each file of each L1 how much of the file was quite. You can see that quite is of course not that frequent in any one file, but, more importantly, the black ‘bars’ at the bottom are the hundreds of files that do not contain quite (massively overplotted grey circles) and the numbers plotted at y=0.009 are the percentages of files per L1 that don’t contain quite:

quite.by.file.perc <- prop.table(quite.by.file, 1)[,2]
l1.by.file <- x$L1[match(
   names(quite.by.file.perc),
   x$FILE)]
plot(0, 0, type="n", xlim=c(0.8, 5.2), ylim=c(0, 0.01), axes=FALSE,
     xlab="L1", ylab="Observed probability of 'quite'"); grid()
   axis(1, at=1:5, labels=levels(x$L1)); axis(2)
   points(pch=16, col="#00000030",
      x=jitter(match(l1.by.file, levels(x$L1))),
      y=quite.by.file.perc)
   text(1:5, 0.009, round(tapply( # text the result of applying
      quite.by.file.perc,         # to the proportions of "quite" per file
      l1.by.file,                 # a grouping by L1
      function (af) mean(af==0)), # to compute the proportion of files
      4))                         # round to 4 decimals

In other words, the data are extremely skewed and this doesn’t look like a data set we just want to force an overall mean per L1 on.1 The problem is in part that the absence of quite can theoretically at least mean different things: It is safe to assume that the native speakers know the word – if they didn’t use it, it’s because they decided not to. But it’s at least theoretically conceivable that a learner might not know the word – a frequency of 0 in a learner file could mean the learner knows the words but didn’t want to use it or it could mean the learner didn’t know the word existed in the first place.

It might therefore be prudent to do a second analysis, one using only the files of the native speakers and those learners that use quite at least once, if only to insure oneself against criticism that one didn’t also check this.

1.4 Analysis 2: learner files with at least 1 quite and native speakers

Ok, let’s (re-)load the packages and the function for the C-score and prepare the data for the second round of modeling:

library(effects); library(emmeans); library(lme4); library(MuMIn)
source("../_helpers/C.score.r")
# let's figure out which files contain "quite" ...
which.files.contain.quite <- sort(
   rownames(quite.by.file)[
      quite.by.file[,2]>0])
# ... & subset as desired:
xx <- subset(x,             # make xx the subset of the rows of w:
   x$L1=="EN" |             # only the rows where the speakers are native speakers OR
   x$FILE %in% which.files.contain.quite) # where the file has at least 1 quite in it

1.4.1 Model fitting/selection

Ok, now that we have the relevant subset of the data, let’s run the model:

summary(m.01.min1 <- glmer(
   QUITE ~ 1 + # QUITE ~ an overall intercept (1)
   L1contr +   # L1contr as a predictor
   (1|FILE),   # w/ varying intercepts for each file
   family=binomial, data=xx), correlation=FALSE)
## Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace Approximation) ['glmerMod']
##  Family: binomial  ( logit )
## Formula: QUITE ~ 1 + L1contr + (1 | FILE)
##    Data: xx
## 
##      AIC      BIC   logLik deviance df.resid 
##   6814.6   6881.5  -3401.3   6802.6   516726 
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##    Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
## -0.048 -0.045 -0.014 -0.014 69.522 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  FILE   (Intercept) 0        0       
## Number of obs: 516732, groups:  FILE, 678
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                             Estimate Std. Error  z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)                 -6.69260    0.04882 -137.083   <2e-16 ***
## L1contrnative_vs_nonnative  -2.23836    0.11905  -18.802   <2e-16 ***
## L1contrromance_vs_germanic  -0.19649    0.10845   -1.812   0.0700 .  
## L1contrspanish_vs_french    -0.42772    0.17077   -2.505   0.0123 *  
## L1contrnorwergian_vs_german -0.13491    0.13460   -1.002   0.3162    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## optimizer (Nelder_Mead) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
saveRDS(m.01.min1, file="../_outputfiles/06_exercises_answerkey_m.01.min1.RDS")
drop1(           # drop each droppable predictor at a time
   m.01.min1,    # from the model m.01.min1 &
   test="Chisq") # test its significance w/ a LR-test
## Single term deletions
## 
## Model:
## QUITE ~ 1 + L1contr + (1 | FILE)
##         npar    AIC    LRT   Pr(Chi)    
## <none>       6814.6                     
## L1contr    4 7222.5 415.89 < 2.2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

So, the model is highly significant (though this one comes with a singularity warning, something we will ignore for now, that’s not the point of this exercise) – still not very surprising given that we have >516K data points. How well does the model do, though?

C.score(m.01.min1)
## [1] 0.7531062
r.squaredGLMM(m.01.min1)
##                    R2m        R2c
## theoretical 0.26683306 0.26683306
## delta       0.00110396 0.00110396

The C-score of 0.753 is below 0.8, but actually better than one might have feared; but R2marginal has improved to 0.267.

1.4.2 Model interpretation

Let’s check the classification accuracy of this model:

xx$PREDS.NUM <- predict( # make xx$PREDS.NUM the result of predicting
   m.01.min1,            # from m.01.min1
   type="response")      # predicted probabilities of "yes"
xx$PREDS.CAT <- factor(      # make xx$PREDS.CAT the result of checking
   ifelse(xx$PREDS.NUM>=0.5, # if the predicted prob of "yes" is >=0.5
      levels(xx$QUITE)[2],   # if yes, predict "yes"
      levels(xx$QUITE)[1]))  # otherwise, predict "no"
(qwe <- table(xx$QUITE, xx$PREDS.CAT)) # confusion matrix & its eval
##      
##           no
##   no  516281
##   yes    451

Same as before: the model never predicts quite, it still has no discriminatory power.

Let’s visualize. We try the normal kind of effects plot first but again not with the complete 0-to-1 range of the y axis that we usually do:

plot(allEffects(m.01.min1), type="response", ylim=c(0, 0.005), grid=TRUE)

And then let’s compute all pairwise comparisons. However, these are not our a priori contrasts, which means now we’re again kinda fishing and so we do correct for multiple post-hoc tests.

pairs( # compute pairwise comparisons
    emmeans(            # of means computed
      object=m.01.min1, # for the model m.01.min1
       ~ L1contr),      # namely all contrasts of this predictor
   adjust="Holm")       # adjust for 3 comparisons
##  contrast estimate    SE  df z.ratio p.value
##  EN - FR   -2.3540 0.154 Inf -15.272  <.0001
##  EN - GE   -2.4041 0.136 Inf -17.631  <.0001
##  EN - NO   -2.2692 0.150 Inf -15.164  <.0001
##  EN - SP   -1.9263 0.165 Inf -11.648  <.0001
##  FR - GE   -0.0501 0.140 Inf  -0.358  1.0000
##  FR - NO    0.0848 0.154 Inf   0.550  1.0000
##  FR - SP    0.4277 0.171 Inf   2.505  0.0613
##  GE - NO    0.1349 0.135 Inf   1.002  0.9485
##  GE - SP    0.4778 0.153 Inf   3.128  0.0105
##  NO - SP    0.3429 0.166 Inf   2.065  0.1556
## 
## Results are given on the log odds ratio (not the response) scale. 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 10 tests

So, bottom line: The overall model is now much more significant than before (LR-test=415.89, df=4, p<0.0001) and a non-trivial R2marginal (≈0.267), but it still has zero discriminatory power and still never predicts quite: accuracy and precision are at baseline, recall is 0, and C is now not great as well.

Remember the authors of the paper actually had not mentioned any hypotheses in their paper. As above that means, they would have top go with the exhaustive comparisons, which show that, with the required control for multiple tests:

  • the native speakers use quite significantly less often than every learner group;
  • the German learners use quite significantly more often than the Spanish learners.

In other words, there is evidence for overuse, but not for the Germanic vs. Romance distinction.

If on the other hand, the authors had stipulated and motivated a priori hypotheses that (i) learners overuse quite and that (ii) there is a Germanic vs. Romance distinction, then they could have gone with the results from the summary table, which are not really working out as one might have hoped for:

  • yes, all non-native speakers use quite more often than all native speakers, but
  • the difference between the two L1 families of romance vs. germanic is not significant and while a p-value of 0.07 might seem ‘close’, one has to bear in mind that the effect has to be really small/volatile if it can’t reach significance with the current sample sizes …;
  • this, together with the facts that Spanish and French learners are significantly different, but Norwegian and German learners are not, is not the best news for a ‘desired’ division of the learners along L1 families.

1.5 Concluding remarks

Counter to what it meay seem like, this discussion is not about bashing Hasselgård & Johansson (2011) or the many other learner corpus studies that apply one or more chi-squared/G2-tests to learner corpus data, but given the many problems this study has and the widespreadedness (if that’s a word) of the problems, it is very instructive. (Plus, I always get these comments and sideways glances implying ‘oh, just shut up, things – (the applications of) statistical methods in linguistics – are not as bad as you always complain!’ Well, I’m not so sure … Thus, I hope it’s clear what to learn from the problems of the study discussed above:

  • don’t use chi-squared tests when their assumptions are violated;
  • describe your methods so that they are replicable;
  • control for multiple post-hoc tests if you don’t have/state any hypotheses;
  • don’t use a combination of hypothesis testing methods and uncorrected multiple testing to try and say something about the data – we have seen above how much the absence or presence of defensible hypotheses affects the interpretation of the results.

Also, I hope this exercise underscored the importance of ‘detective work’, here in the case of tabulating and plotting to figure out that the vast majority of texts didn’t even contain the item in question, meaning the first analysis is based on extremely skewed data. In fact, there’s one other thing that is interesting here: look at the plot again of all files’ proportions of quite – anything in there that makes you think of something?

THINK BREAK

I am glad you noticed the one absolute extreme outlier: the German speaker represented in FILE: GEDR1013, who used quite 9 times in 877 words, i.e. more than 1%. Remember our discussion of influence measures/diagnostics? That one speaker has a huge impact on, e.g. the coefficient L1contrnorwergian_vs_german. If that speaker gets omitted (from the version of the data that contain only the learners that use quite at least once), that coefficient decreases by more than 37%, meaning this is a data set for which validation/bootstrapping and influence measures are probably useful. Like I said, always check your stuff as carefully as you can!

Final thing I want to briefly mention here, something I discuss in the article on which this exercise is based: One big general weakness of all kinds of such over-/underuse studies is that, even if you do a fancier model on them like we did, they are ultimately always going to be really bad – why? Because they are zero- or monofactorial (depending on what conceptual role L1 plays)! Even our mixed-effects model had only one predictor – L1. I’m just gonna come out and say it: How useful can any such work possibly be?! We’re pretending that the use of a certain lexical item can be explained well just by looking at the L1s of the speakers, with no other contextual predictors? With no controls (such as text length, proficiency, lexical diversity/sophistication? Over-/underuse without any other linguistic, cognitive, individual-variation, or SLA-related predictors are simply going to be dull because nothing meaningful can be said about a lexical choice when all the linguistic and psychological context of the choices is just ignored – we need more predictors and controls. If you were to read up on the paper on which this exercise is based, you will see that even just adding text length (!), lexical diversity, and lexical dispersion makers the resulting models much better and Section 3.2 of that paper then discusses what I think we need (esp. Sections 3.2.2 and 3.2.3).

2 Parviainen & Fuchs (2018)

Parviainen & Fuchs (2018) is a study on the degree to which the use of the additive clause-final particle also and the restrictive clause-final particle only (as exemplified in (their) examples 1 and 2) in Hong Kong English (HKE) and Philippine English (PhiE) might be due to the influence of the Indian English (IndE) epicenter:

  1. I do not have to work also (ICE-HK:S1A-004#967)
  2. I don’t get time only (ICE-IND:S1A-052#37)

This clause-final positioning of the particles is possible, but dispreferred, in Inner Circle varieties, where generally a clause-medial position between subject and verb is preferred, but it is attested in many Asian Englishes and particularly frequent in IndE. After ruling out an explanation of these particles’ distributions in HKE and PhiE based on substrates and given the absence of diachronic spoken corpora covering the varieties of interest, Parviainen & Fuchs adopt the sociolinguistic apparent-time method, according to which “speakers from different age groups can be argued to represent the type of language that was used in their adolescence” (p. 6). Specifically, they state (p. 2) that

By examining the age and gender of those who use innovative also and only in ICE-IND, ICE-PHI and ICE-HK, it is possible to estimate how established the feature is in each variety and, consequently, provide further evidence on the potential influence of IndE in the Southeast Asian region. If clause-final also and only are used more frequently by younger speakers than by older speakers and/or more frequently by female speakers than by male speakers, we can infer that these features are becoming more frequent in these varieties.

Then, in their methods section on p. 7, they follow the above up with this:

Therefore, if the relative proportion of the use of innovative also and only by young (and) female speakers is higher than that of older (and) male speakers in both PhiE and HKE (when compared with IndE), this would indicate that the use of the feature is a more recent innovation in the former two varieties. This, in turn, would lend further support to the argument that IndE is an emerging epicentre in South(east) Asia.

Their analysis is then based on the private-conversation parts of the studied components – ICE-HK, ICE-Ind, and ICE-Phi – of the International Corpus of English (ICE), for which relevant metadata for the speakers are available. They retrieved all instances of clause-final also/only from these corpus parts and put together a data frame that included the following variables:

  • AGE: a predictor variable with the 4 levels [0, 25], [26, 35], [36, 50], and [50, ∞];
  • GENDER: a binary predictor variable with the levels female and male;
  • VARIETY: a ternary variable with the levels HKE, IndE, and PhiE;
  • PARTICLE: a binary variable with the levels also and only;
  • PMW: a numeric response variable with the relative frequencies (per million words) of clause-final positions uses of the two particles for all speakers in each of the 4×2×3×2=48 combinations of all levels of AGE, GENDER, VARIETY, and PARTICLE. (The formulation in the paper from which I inferred what I just described is this: “The final step in the analysis consisted of counting the number of tokens of clause-final also and only uttered by speakers of the different age and gender groups in each corpus, and counting the number of words contributed to the corpus by each of these groups.”)

Let’s load the data:

rm(list=ls(all.names=TRUE))
library(car); library(effects); library(emmeans); library(MASS)
head(x <- read.delim("../_inputfiles/06_also-only-ex.csv", stringsAsFactors=TRUE))
##   CASE PARTICLE   AGE GENDER SPKRS VARIETY TOKENS  WORDS PMWpaper
## 1    1     also 14–25 female    84     HKE     45 107796      417
## 2    2     also 26–35 female     3     HKE      0   5304        0
## 3    3     also 36–50 female     6     HKE      0   5888        0
## 4    4     also   >50 female     0     HKE      0      0        0
## 5    5     also 14–25   male    15     HKE      5  16599      301
## 6    6     also 26–35   male     2     HKE      0   2892        0

For our analyses later, we will not use the values of PMWpaper, which are the values they provide in the paper, but the exact ones computed from TOKENS and WORDS:

x$PMW <- (x$TOKENS/x$WORDS) * 1E6; x[is.na(x)] <- 0
summary(x)
##       CASE       PARTICLE     AGE        GENDER       SPKRS        VARIETY       TOKENS    
##  Min.   : 1.00   also:24   >50  :12   female:24   Min.   :  0.00   HKE :16   Min.   : 0.0  
##  1st Qu.:12.75   only:24   14–25:12   male  :24   1st Qu.:  3.75   IndE:16   1st Qu.: 0.0  
##  Median :24.50             26–35:12               Median : 26.50   PhiE:16   Median : 5.0  
##  Mean   :24.50             36–50:12               Mean   : 37.85             Mean   :11.6  
##  3rd Qu.:36.25                                    3rd Qu.: 57.75             3rd Qu.:17.0  
##  Max.   :48.00                                    Max.   :170.00             Max.   :74.0  
##      WORDS           PMWpaper           PMW        
##  Min.   :     0   Min.   :   0.0   Min.   :   0.0  
##  1st Qu.:  4962   1st Qu.:   0.0   1st Qu.:   0.0  
##  Median : 15418   Median : 261.5   Median : 261.5  
##  Mean   : 22786   Mean   : 412.0   Mean   : 412.0  
##  3rd Qu.: 30074   3rd Qu.: 673.8   3rd Qu.: 674.1  
##  Max.   :107796   Max.   :1705.0   Max.   :1704.7

To describe their statistical analysis and results, let me quote their study extensively so you see exactly what they say (I’m using my usual typographic conventions for variable names etc.) and what they arrived at:

[The above-mentioned way of creating PMW] allowed us to calculate the relative frequency of the two features per million words (pmw), and to apply linear regression models in order to determine: whether younger and/or female speakers are more likely to use the feature in question; and whether the three varieties differ in this regard as well as in the overall frequencies of clause-final only and also. Two regression models were computed in R with relative frequency of clause-final only and also, respectively, as dependent variables, and [AGE, GENDER and VARIETY] as independent variables. Model selection was conducted using the step function, with F-tests as the selection criterion, and allowed for interactions of up to three variables. After model selection, post-hoc Tukey tests (corrected for multiple comparisons) were conducted with the lsmeans function from the eponymous R package (Lenth & Hervé, 2015).

Then, here’s how they summarize the results for also in prose (again adjusted in terms of formatting, and obviously you should seize the opportunity and re-create the plots):

For clause-final also, the regression model involved a main effect for independent variables VARIETY and GENDER, as well as an interaction between these factors, but no main effect for AGE. As Figure 1 illustrates […; see below for my approximation], this focus particle is much more frequent in IndE than in PhiE (female t=6.3, p<0.0001; male t=3.7, p<0.01; df=18 for these and all following tests) and HKE (female t=8.5, p<0.0001; male t=5.5, p=0.0001). It is also more frequent in PhiE than in HKE, where it is in fact used exclusively by speakers below the age of 26 years, but the difference fails to reach significance (female t=2.1, p=0.11; male t=1.7, p=0.22). Clause-final also is more frequent in female speech across all age groups in IndE, in all but one group in PhiE and in one group in HKE (as the focus particle was not used by any speaker above the age of 25 years). Nevertheless, the difference is only significant in IndE (t=3.2, p<0.01), but not in PhiE (t=0.6, p=0.57) or HKE (t=0.2, p=0.86). While AGE was not included in the regression model as an influential factor, a glance at the data reveals that, in HKE, clause-final also is used exclusively by speakers from the youngest group. In IndE and PhiE, by contrast, it is equally frequent in all AGE groups.

And here’s how they summarize the corresponding results for only in prose:

For clause-final only, the regression model involved a main effect for independent variables VARIETY and AGE, as well as an interaction between these factors, but no main effect for GENDER. This focus particle is much more frequent in IndE than in both PhiE and HKE in all but the oldest group of speakers (see [my approximation of their figure below]). This difference is significant in the youngest group of speakers, aged 14-25 years (IndE vs. PhiE t=6.7, p<0.0001; IndE vs. HKE t=6.0, p<0.001; df=12 for these and all following tests), the group aged 26-35 years (IndE vs. PhiE t=3.8, p<0.01; IndE vs. HKE t=2.8, p<0.05) and the group aged 36-50 years (IndE vs. PhiE t=2.9, p<0.05; IndE vs. HKE t=3.2, p<0.05). Among speakers above 50 years of age, the focus particle is more frequent in IndE than in HKE, but not significantly so (t=0.7, p=0.8), and is just as frequent in IndE as in PhiE (t=0.1, p=0.99).

As for speaker AGE, we observe a clear age trend in IndE, with clause-final only decreasing almost steadily in frequency from the youngest to the oldest group of speakers. It is significantly more frequent in the youngest group of speakers compared to those aged 26-35 years (t=3.0, p<0.05), 36-50 years (t=3.9, p<0.01) and older than 50 years (t=6.4, p<0.001). The difference between those aged 26-35 and 36-50 years is not significant (t=0.9, p=0.80), but that between the 26-35 and 50+ age groups is (t=3.4, p<0.05). Finally, the difference between the 36-50 and 50+ age groups fails to reach significance (t=2.5, p=0.12). In HKE, we also observe an age trend, as the focus particle was found only in the speech of the two youngest groups and was not used by older speakers. However, the differences between the groups do not reach significance. Finally, in PhiE there is no age trend, as clause-final only is used at a low frequency across all age groups. While GENDER was not included in the regression model as an influential factor, a glance at the data reveals that, in HKE, clause-final ‘also’ [I think they mean only here, STG] is more frequent in the language of female compared to male speakers. In IndE and PhiE, by contrast, the analysis of speaker GENDER reveals no clear trend. The focus particle is more frequent in the language of female speakers in some age groups, and more frequent in the language of male speakers in others.

In addition, they provide an overview table (their Table 1) summarizing their results:

Clause-final Effect IndE HKE PhiE
also AGE: younger > older – (n.s.) –
also GENDER: female > male (p<0.01) (n.s.) (n.s.)
only AGE: younger > older (up to p<0.001) (n.s.) –
only GENDER: female > male – (n.s.) –

Commendably, Parviainen & Fuchs (2018) provide the data they used for their models in an appendix to the paper.[^While P&F provide their data, there seem to be small errors in their data as reported in Appendix 1. First, there is a data point, x[20,], where they report to have no speakers (more on that below), but they still report 2 uses of also, which seems to be impossible by definition. Second, if you compare x[22,] to x[46,], you can see that the frequency of male speakers of PhiE between 26 and 35 years of age is once reported as 75 and once as 78. Thus, even if we were able to replicate their results, they are most likely incorrect.] Your tasks? Simple and as above for Hasselgård & Johansson (2011): (i) review the analysis from a statistical perspective, but thoroughly, like your tenure case depends on it (there is a lot you should be picking up on, it took me days to write the answer key to this exercise, but it’s really instructive) and (ii) discuss how the data might be analyzed better.[^The conceptual interpretation of the results is actually also interesting but in this statistics-textbook context I will not comment on that part of the paper.]

Unfortunately, I think the analysis P&F are reporting is nevertheless fraught with several issues of different degrees of severity (even as I acknowledge openly that I’ve been trying to make clear that modeling comes with many ‘researcher degrees of freedom’ and there are many different decisions to be made, not all of which everyone would make the same way – there isn’t always an obvious, right way). In what follows below, I will provide (i) my take on their analysis, focusing on the things that I am more certain than not should have been done better, and (ii) talk about some things that one should bear in mind when analyzing this data set (where I’m not implying that P&F didn’t do these things: Even if they’re not mentioned in the paper, they might have still looked at those – I’m just mentioning them here because, in this textbook context, I want to remind you of them).

Let me start with the bottom line: Even disregarding for now the errors in their data mentioned in the footnote above, I personally think it is actually unclear whether the findings they report can be taken at face value even by someone who re-runs their analysis, which is what we’ll begin with. Note that in all that follows I will not be concerned with the fact that fitting a linear model on a response variables whose values can only be ≥0 can also be problematic.

2.1 Replicating their analyses

First, we will prepare some of the variables for the subsequent modeling: We make IndE the reference level of VARIETY so the coefficients in the summary of our models will compare IndE – the presumed epicenter – to the other two varieties.

x$VARIETY <- factor(x$VARIETY, levels=levels(x$VARIETY)[c(2,1,3)])
summary(x)
##       CASE       PARTICLE     AGE        GENDER       SPKRS        VARIETY       TOKENS    
##  Min.   : 1.00   also:24   >50  :12   female:24   Min.   :  0.00   IndE:16   Min.   : 0.0  
##  1st Qu.:12.75   only:24   14–25:12   male  :24   1st Qu.:  3.75   HKE :16   1st Qu.: 0.0  
##  Median :24.50             26–35:12               Median : 26.50   PhiE:16   Median : 5.0  
##  Mean   :24.50             36–50:12               Mean   : 37.85             Mean   :11.6  
##  3rd Qu.:36.25                                    3rd Qu.: 57.75             3rd Qu.:17.0  
##  Max.   :48.00                                    Max.   :170.00             Max.   :74.0  
##      WORDS           PMWpaper           PMW        
##  Min.   :     0   Min.   :   0.0   Min.   :   0.0  
##  1st Qu.:  4962   1st Qu.:   0.0   1st Qu.:   0.0  
##  Median : 15418   Median : 261.5   Median : 261.5  
##  Mean   : 22786   Mean   : 412.0   Mean   : 412.0  
##  3rd Qu.: 30074   3rd Qu.: 673.8   3rd Qu.: 674.1  
##  Max.   :107796   Max.   :1705.0   Max.   :1704.7

Also, we will change the order of levels of AGE to something more sensible:

x$AGE <- factor(x$AGE, levels=levels(x$AGE)[c(2:4, 1)])

Let’s quickly check the distribution of the response with hist(x$PMW, main="") (plot not shown here). This looks like it might result in problems, but maybe it’ll work nonetheless once we do our model diagnostics. To replicate their analyses, we need to split up the data by particle:

xx <- split(x, x$PARTICLE, drop=TRUE)

Let’s replicate the also part first:

2.1.1 Modeling also

P&F mention they used step, which suggests an automatic model selection process, which, for observational data at least I am usually not fond of, but given their design, collinearity will not be a problem. However, they specifically said they used “the step function, with F-tests as the selection criterion”, which I found surprising, because, as far as I know at least, step is using AIC, not F in its decisions so, in a review, I would ask about that. Thankfully, when we use step, we arrive at the same final model as they did (at least it seems that way, we do not get to see any summary tables …):

summary(m.final.also <- stepAIC(
   lm(PMW ~ 1, data=xx$also),
   scope=list(
      lower= ~1,
      upper= ~AGE*GENDER*VARIETY),
   trace=0)) # do not show all the steps, set to 1 or omit for more details
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + GENDER + VARIETY:GENDER, data = xx$also)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -388.46  -98.86  -65.25  101.85  608.34 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                        Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)              1508.8      117.4  12.856 1.65e-10 ***
## VARIETYHKE              -1404.4      166.0  -8.462 1.09e-07 ***
## VARIETYPhiE             -1052.9      166.0  -6.344 5.61e-06 ***
## GENDERmale               -526.8      166.0  -3.174  0.00526 ** 
## VARIETYHKE:GENDERmale     497.7      234.7   2.120  0.04813 *  
## VARIETYPhiE:GENDERmale    431.9      234.7   1.840  0.08232 .  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 234.7 on 18 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8635, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8256 
## F-statistic: 22.78 on 5 and 18 DF,  p-value: 3.33e-07

This is a pretty good R2, which I think they should have reported as an indicator of model quality (though see below). What’s the p-value of the interaction they discuss?

drop1(           # drop each droppable predictor at a time
   m.final.also, # from the model m.final.also &
   test="F")     # test its significance w/ an F-test
## Single term deletions
## 
## Model:
## PMW ~ VARIETY + GENDER + VARIETY:GENDER
##                Df Sum of Sq     RSS   AIC F value  Pr(>F)  
## <none>                       991668 267.1                  
## VARIETY:GENDER  2    292369 1284037 269.3  2.6534 0.09775 .
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Ok, that’s strange: As mentioned above, they say they did model selection using step with F-tests, but the F-test actually shows that their interaction is ns – it seems like (speculation!) they used step thinking it uses F-tests but the interaction is really only retained in the final model because AIC recommended that, not because an F-test did! This is definitely something to be mentioned in a review.

Obviously, we can compute confidence intervals, which I am not showing here.

Confint(m.final.also) # return estimates w/ their 95%-CI

Let’s compute the predictions

vg.df.also<- data.frame( # make vg.df.also a data frame of
   vg.also <- effect(    # an object called vg.also: the effect
      "VARIETY:GENDER",  # of VARIETY:GENDER
      m.final.also))     # in m.final.also
vg.also # either you now look at vg.also ...
## 
##  VARIETY*GENDER effect
##        GENDER
## VARIETY    female      male
##    IndE 1508.7715 982.02055
##    HKE   104.3638  75.30574
##    PhiE  455.8341 360.96805
vg.df.also # or at the usual data frame vg.df.also
##   VARIETY GENDER        fit       se     lower     upper
## 1    IndE female 1508.77152 117.3591 1262.2091 1755.3339
## 2     HKE female  104.36380 117.3591 -142.1986  350.9262
## 3    PhiE female  455.83412 117.3591  209.2717  702.3965
## 4    IndE   male  982.02055 117.3591  735.4581 1228.5830
## 5     HKE   male   75.30574 117.3591 -171.2567  321.8682
## 6    PhiE   male  360.96805 117.3591  114.4056  607.5305

They then report using lsmeans for post-hoc testing. Unfortunately, they don’t state unambiguously in the methods part which of the following 3 approaches they used, but from the results they report, we can piece together that they used the first two (which is good); we’re using the newer, more actively maintained emmeans just like in the book:

# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.also
# of VARIETY within each level of GENDER, adjust using Tukey
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.also, ~ VARIETY | GENDER), adjust="Tukey")
## GENDER = female:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE      1404 166 18   8.462  <.0001
##  IndE - PhiE     1053 166 18   6.344  <.0001
##  HKE - PhiE      -351 166 18  -2.118  0.1143
## 
## GENDER = male:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE       907 166 18   5.463  0.0001
##  IndE - PhiE      621 166 18   3.742  0.0041
##  HKE - PhiE      -286 166 18  -1.721  0.2247
## 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates
# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.also
# of GENDER within each level of VARIETY, adjust using Tukey
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.also, ~ GENDER | VARIETY), adjust="Tukey")
## VARIETY = IndE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  female - male    526.8 166 18   3.174  0.0053
## 
## VARIETY = HKE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  female - male     29.1 166 18   0.175  0.8630
## 
## VARIETY = PhiE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  female - male     94.9 166 18   0.572  0.5747
# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.also
# of all comparisons of all combinations of VARIETY:GENDER, adjust using Tukey
# pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.also, ~ VARIETY:GENDER), adjust="Tukey")

However, what I personally think is less good is that:

  • the dot charts they show for the results (their Figures 1 and 2) include AGE although, as they themselves say, AGE is not in their final model – why do model selection (that eliminates AGE) but then show a graph that leaves it in and, thus, contains more info than is supported by the model? This makes the graph much more complex than it needs to be because the reader has to constantly ‘average across the 4 levels of AGE in the plot’ when we were just told that those don’t matter;
  • the prose also discusses effects of AGE when, again, we were just told it’s actually not in the final model;
  • also, the dot charts are observed values, not predicted ones. While the statistical design is perfectly orthogonal, if I have a final model of the type Y ~ A*B (as they do), I want to see model predictions for A:B, not observed data for A:B:C (esp. also since level conflation of predictors can quickly unorthogonalize – if there is such a word – a design).

The way I would do this – surprise, surprise! – is like I discuss it in the book:

plot(vg.also, x.var="GENDER", ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.also,
      ~ GENDER | VARIETY), adjust="holm") # of GENDER within each level of VARIETY
## VARIETY = IndE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  female - male    526.8 166 18   3.174  0.0053
## 
## VARIETY = HKE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  female - male     29.1 166 18   0.175  0.8630
## 
## VARIETY = PhiE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  female - male     94.9 166 18   0.572  0.5747
plot(vg.also, x.var="VARIETY", ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.also,
      ~ VARIETY | GENDER), adjust="holm") # of VARIETY within each level of GENDER
## GENDER = female:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE      1404 166 18   8.462  <.0001
##  IndE - PhiE     1053 166 18   6.344  <.0001
##  HKE - PhiE      -351 166 18  -2.118  0.0484
## 
## GENDER = male:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE       907 166 18   5.463  0.0001
##  IndE - PhiE      621 166 18   3.742  0.0030
##  HKE - PhiE      -286 166 18  -1.721  0.1024
## 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 3 tests

And, finally, a quick diagnostic check, for which, since I don’t have all year, I’m just using gvlma::gvlma (see Peña & Slate 2012):

gvlma::gvlma(m.final.also)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + GENDER + VARIETY:GENDER, data = xx$also)
## 
## Coefficients:
##            (Intercept)              VARIETYHKE             VARIETYPhiE              GENDERmale  
##                 1508.8                 -1404.4                 -1052.9                  -526.8  
##  VARIETYHKE:GENDERmale  VARIETYPhiE:GENDERmale  
##                  497.7                   431.9  
## 
## 
## ASSESSMENT OF THE LINEAR MODEL ASSUMPTIONS
## USING THE GLOBAL TEST ON 4 DEGREES-OF-FREEDOM:
## Level of Significance =  0.05 
## 
## Call:
##  gvlma::gvlma(x = m.final.also) 
## 
##                        Value p-value                Decision
## Global Stat        8.541e+00 0.07366 Assumptions acceptable.
## Skewness           2.570e+00 0.10892 Assumptions acceptable.
## Kurtosis           2.883e+00 0.08955 Assumptions acceptable.
## Link Function      3.619e-16 1.00000 Assumptions acceptable.
## Heteroscedasticity 3.089e+00 0.07884 Assumptions acceptable.

Looking good, in spite of what might have been a problematic distribution of the response variable.

And then I would write up this analysis (based on how I did the above, based in turn on how I understood P&F; as you’ll see, this is much shorter than their summary although it actually contains more model summary information): An automatic bidirectional model selection process using step (and, thus, using AIC as a selection criterion) was applied to the also part of the data with PMW as the response variable. The minimal model permitted was a model with only an intercept, the maximal model included all 3 predictors AGE, GENDER, and VARIETY and all possible interactions between them. The final model returned by step resulted in a very good fit (F=22.78, df1=5, df2=18, p<0.0001, with mult. R2=0.864, adj. R2=0.826) with unproblematic model diagnostics. The predictor(s) to be interpreted in the final model is the interaction VARIETY:GENDER (which is only included because AIC was used: pdeletion=0.09775). Multiple post-hoc testing with Holm’s correction shows the nature of the interaction:

  • in IndE, women use clause-final also more than men (t=3.174, df=18, pHolm=0.0053) – no differences between women and men were found in the other varieties;
  • both IndE women and men use clause-final also more than women and men of the other varieties (all p≤0.003), plus there is a just about significant difference (t=-2.118, df=18, pHolm=0.0484) such that women speaking HKE use it more than women speaking PhiE.

2.1.2 Modeling only

summary(m.final.only <- stepAIC(
   lm(PMW ~ 1, data=xx$only),
   scope=list(
      lower= ~1,
      upper= ~AGE*GENDER*VARIETY),
   trace=0)) # do not show all the steps, set to 1 or omit for more details
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + AGE + VARIETY:AGE, data = xx$only)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -192.41  -74.38    0.00   74.38  192.41 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)            1069.3      106.8  10.011 3.54e-07 ***
## VARIETYHKE             -904.7      151.1  -5.989 6.32e-05 ***
## VARIETYPhiE           -1004.9      151.1  -6.653 2.35e-05 ***
## AGE26–35               -450.7      151.1  -2.984 0.011401 *  
## AGE36–50               -589.3      151.1  -3.901 0.002105 ** 
## AGE>50                 -959.7      151.1  -6.353 3.65e-05 ***
## VARIETYHKE:AGE26–35     474.6      213.6   2.222 0.046281 *  
## VARIETYPhiE:AGE26–35    435.2      213.6   2.037 0.064288 .  
## VARIETYHKE:AGE36–50     424.7      213.6   1.988 0.070118 .  
## VARIETYPhiE:AGE36–50    567.8      213.6   2.658 0.020877 *  
## VARIETYHKE:AGE>50       795.1      213.6   3.722 0.002917 ** 
## VARIETYPhiE:AGE>50     1022.3      213.6   4.786 0.000444 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 151.1 on 12 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8935, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7958 
## F-statistic: 9.149 on 11 and 12 DF,  p-value: 0.0003006

Again pretty good R2s, which I think they should have reported. What’s the p-value of the interaction they discuss?

drop1(           # drop each droppable predictor at a time
   m.final.only, # from the model m.final.only &
   test="F")     # test its significance w/ an F-test
## Single term deletions
## 
## Model:
## PMW ~ VARIETY + AGE + VARIETY:AGE
##             Df Sum of Sq    RSS    AIC F value  Pr(>F)  
## <none>                   273810 248.21                  
## VARIETY:AGE  6    598507 872316 264.02  4.3717 0.01435 *
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Ok, this time the interaction they found (presumably using AIC) is also significant, good. Again, we can compute confidence intervals, which I am not showing here.

Confint(m.final.only) # return estimates w/ their 95%-CI

Let’s compute the predictions

va.df.only <- data.frame( # make va.df.only a data frame of
   va.only <- effect(     # an object called va.only: the effect
      "VARIETY:AGE",      # of VARIETY:AGE
      m.final.only))      # in m.final.only
va.only # either you now look at va.only ...
## 
##  VARIETY*AGE effect
##        AGE
## VARIETY      14–25     26–35        36–50          >50
##    IndE 1069.31925 618.57401 4.800056e+02 1.096251e+02
##    HKE   164.63564 188.53695 5.684342e-14 2.273737e-13
##    PhiE   64.37975  48.86398 4.283022e+01 1.270003e+02
va.df.only # or at the usual data frame va.df.only
##    VARIETY   AGE          fit       se      lower     upper
## 1     IndE 14–25 1.069319e+03 106.8117  836.59658 1302.0419
## 2      HKE 14–25 1.646356e+02 106.8117  -68.08703  397.3583
## 3     PhiE 14–25 6.437975e+01 106.8117 -168.34291  297.1024
## 4     IndE 26–35 6.185740e+02 106.8117  385.85134  851.2967
## 5      HKE 26–35 1.885370e+02 106.8117  -44.18571  421.2596
## 6     PhiE 26–35 4.886398e+01 106.8117 -183.85868  281.5866
## 7     IndE 36–50 4.800056e+02 106.8117  247.28290  712.7282
## 8      HKE 36–50 5.684342e-14 106.8117 -232.72267  232.7227
## 9     PhiE 36–50 4.283022e+01 106.8117 -189.89245  275.5529
## 10    IndE   >50 1.096251e+02 106.8117 -123.09758  342.3477
## 11     HKE   >50 2.273737e-13 106.8117 -232.72267  232.7227
## 12    PhiE   >50 1.270003e+02 106.8117 -105.72241  359.7229

We can then do the post-hoc testing; again I’m using Holm’s correction:

# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.only
# of VARIETY within each level of AGE, adjust using Tukey
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.only, ~ VARIETY | AGE), adjust="Tukey")
## AGE = 14–25:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     904.7 151 12   5.989  0.0002
##  IndE - PhiE   1004.9 151 12   6.653  0.0001
##  HKE - PhiE     100.3 151 12   0.664  0.7883
## 
## AGE = 26–35:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     430.0 151 12   2.847  0.0364
##  IndE - PhiE    569.7 151 12   3.772  0.0070
##  HKE - PhiE     139.7 151 12   0.925  0.6358
## 
## AGE = 36–50:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     480.0 151 12   3.178  0.0202
##  IndE - PhiE    437.2 151 12   2.894  0.0335
##  HKE - PhiE     -42.8 151 12  -0.284  0.9568
## 
## AGE = >50:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     109.6 151 12   0.726  0.7533
##  IndE - PhiE    -17.4 151 12  -0.115  0.9927
##  HKE - PhiE    -127.0 151 12  -0.841  0.6859
## 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates
# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.only
# of AGE within each level of VARIETY, adjust using Tukey
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.only, ~ AGE | VARIETY), adjust="Tukey")
## VARIETY = IndE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35   450.75 151 12   2.984  0.0487
##  14–25 - 36–50   589.31 151 12   3.901  0.0098
##  14–25 - >50     959.69 151 12   6.353  0.0002
##  26–35 - 36–50   138.57 151 12   0.917  0.7962
##  26–35 - >50     508.95 151 12   3.369  0.0249
##  36–50 - >50     370.38 151 12   2.452  0.1195
## 
## VARIETY = HKE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35   -23.90 151 12  -0.158  0.9985
##  14–25 - 36–50   164.64 151 12   1.090  0.7020
##  14–25 - >50     164.64 151 12   1.090  0.7020
##  26–35 - 36–50   188.54 151 12   1.248  0.6102
##  26–35 - >50     188.54 151 12   1.248  0.6102
##  36–50 - >50       0.00 151 12   0.000  1.0000
## 
## VARIETY = PhiE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35    15.52 151 12   0.103  0.9996
##  14–25 - 36–50    21.55 151 12   0.143  0.9989
##  14–25 - >50     -62.62 151 12  -0.415  0.9749
##  26–35 - 36–50     6.03 151 12   0.040  1.0000
##  26–35 - >50     -78.14 151 12  -0.517  0.9533
##  36–50 - >50     -84.17 151 12  -0.557  0.9428
## 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 4 estimates
# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.only
# of all comparisons of all combinations of VARIETY:AGE, adjust using Tukey
# pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.only, ~ VARIETY:AGE), adjust="Tukey")

I have the same problems with the visualization as before:

  • the dot charts they show for the results include GENDER although, as they themselves say, GENDER is not a predictor in their final model – why do model selection then? Therefore, the graph is much more complex than it needs to be because the reader has to constantly ‘average across the 2 levels of GENDER in the plot’ when we were just told those don’t matter;
  • the prose also discusses effects of GENDER when, again, we were just told it’s not part of the final model;
  • also, the dot charts are observed values, not predicted ones; see above.

So again I would do this like this:

plot(va.only, x.var="AGE", ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.only,
      ~ AGE | VARIETY), adjust="holm") # of AGE within each level of VARIETY
## VARIETY = IndE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35   450.75 151 12   2.984  0.0342
##  14–25 - 36–50   589.31 151 12   3.901  0.0105
##  14–25 - >50     959.69 151 12   6.353  0.0002
##  26–35 - 36–50   138.57 151 12   0.917  0.3770
##  26–35 - >50     508.95 151 12   3.369  0.0223
##  36–50 - >50     370.38 151 12   2.452  0.0610
## 
## VARIETY = HKE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35   -23.90 151 12  -0.158  1.0000
##  14–25 - 36–50   164.64 151 12   1.090  1.0000
##  14–25 - >50     164.64 151 12   1.090  1.0000
##  26–35 - 36–50   188.54 151 12   1.248  1.0000
##  26–35 - >50     188.54 151 12   1.248  1.0000
##  36–50 - >50       0.00 151 12   0.000  1.0000
## 
## VARIETY = PhiE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35    15.52 151 12   0.103  1.0000
##  14–25 - 36–50    21.55 151 12   0.143  1.0000
##  14–25 - >50     -62.62 151 12  -0.415  1.0000
##  26–35 - 36–50     6.03 151 12   0.040  1.0000
##  26–35 - >50     -78.14 151 12  -0.517  1.0000
##  36–50 - >50     -84.17 151 12  -0.557  1.0000
## 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 6 tests
plot(va.only, x.var="VARIETY", ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.only,
      ~ VARIETY | AGE), adjust="holm") # of VARIETY within each level of AGE
## AGE = 14–25:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     904.7 151 12   5.989  0.0001
##  IndE - PhiE   1004.9 151 12   6.653  0.0001
##  HKE - PhiE     100.3 151 12   0.664  0.5194
## 
## AGE = 26–35:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     430.0 151 12   2.847  0.0294
##  IndE - PhiE    569.7 151 12   3.772  0.0080
##  HKE - PhiE     139.7 151 12   0.925  0.3734
## 
## AGE = 36–50:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     480.0 151 12   3.178  0.0239
##  IndE - PhiE    437.2 151 12   2.894  0.0269
##  HKE - PhiE     -42.8 151 12  -0.284  0.7816
## 
## AGE = >50:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     109.6 151 12   0.726  1.0000
##  IndE - PhiE    -17.4 151 12  -0.115  1.0000
##  HKE - PhiE    -127.0 151 12  -0.841  1.0000
## 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 3 tests

[Two quick side remarks: (i) P&F treat their binned version of AGE as a categorical predictor when, of course, it is an ordinal predictor. Therefore, I think a case could, perhaps, be made to not consider (and correct for) all 6 comparisons that can be made between the 4 ordinal levels of AGE but just the 3 successive differences comparisons, but the really better apporach would of course be to use successive differences comparisons anyway with contr.sdif. (Alternatively, it might be interesting to test for conflation of age groups – do we really need all 4?) (ii) These two effects plots are, I think, another great example why one should always plot not just perspective of an interaction: the former makes the difference between IndE and the other two soooo as well as the timeline much more obvious than the latter …]

And, finally, the same quick diagnostic check, which again seems fine:

gvlma::gvlma(m.final.only)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + AGE + VARIETY:AGE, data = xx$only)
## 
## Coefficients:
##          (Intercept)            VARIETYHKE           VARIETYPhiE              AGE26–35  
##               1069.3                -904.7               -1004.9                -450.7  
##             AGE36–50                AGE>50   VARIETYHKE:AGE26–35  VARIETYPhiE:AGE26–35  
##               -589.3                -959.7                 474.6                 435.2  
##  VARIETYHKE:AGE36–50  VARIETYPhiE:AGE36–50     VARIETYHKE:AGE>50    VARIETYPhiE:AGE>50  
##                424.7                 567.8                 795.1                1022.3  
## 
## 
## ASSESSMENT OF THE LINEAR MODEL ASSUMPTIONS
## USING THE GLOBAL TEST ON 4 DEGREES-OF-FREEDOM:
## Level of Significance =  0.05 
## 
## Call:
##  gvlma::gvlma(x = m.final.only) 
## 
##                        Value p-value                Decision
## Global Stat        1.166e+00  0.8837 Assumptions acceptable.
## Skewness           1.232e-30  1.0000 Assumptions acceptable.
## Kurtosis           6.421e-01  0.4230 Assumptions acceptable.
## Link Function      4.938e-16  1.0000 Assumptions acceptable.
## Heteroscedasticity 5.236e-01  0.4693 Assumptions acceptable.

Writing this up (again much shorter than P&F): An automatic bidirectional model selection process using step (and, thus, using AIC as a selection criterion) was applied to the only part of the data with PMW as the response variable. The minimal model permitted was a model with only an intercept, the maximal model included all 3 predictors AGE, GENDER, and VARIETY and all possible interactions between them. The final model returned by step resulted in a very good fit (F=9.15, df1=11, df2=12, p<0.0004, with mult. R2=0.893, adj. R2=0.796) with unproblematic model diagnostics. The predictor(s) to be interpreted in the final model is the interaction VARIETY:AGE (pdeletion=0.01435). Multiple post-hoc testing with Holm’s correction shows the nature of the interaction:

  • in IndE, there is a monotonic trend such that the older the speakers, the fewer uses of clause-final only are observed; the post-hoc tests suggest 3 age divisions: 14–25 vs. 26–35/36–50 vs. >50; no differences between any age groups are observed in HKE or PhiE;
  • the younger the speakers, the more differences between varieties can be observed. For instance, in the youngest group, the difference between IndE and the other varieties is most pronounced, but in the oldest group, there are hardly any differences anymore.

Ok, so we’re done replicating. For the most part, we can replicate their analyses although biased me would also think we have improved on them a little bit. But if that’s the case, why did I say above “it is actually unclear whether the findings they report can be taken at face value”? That’s what we’re turning to now …

2.2 Critique & considering improvements

This paper should have triggered multiple sections of the book in your mind, especially,

  1. various sections in chapter 5 and maybe especially section 5.2.3.3
  2. Sections 5.2.4 and 5.2.8;
  3. actually all of Chapter 6 (after all, this is an exercise for Chapter 6 even though the paper didn’t do mixed-effects modeling) but especially the first exercise here on quite.

With regard to the first point, we need to inspect the way they formulate their expectations(/hypotheses?). Remember, they said

By examining the age and gender of those who use innovative also and only in ICE-IND, ICE-PHI and ICE-HK, it is possible to estimate how established the feature is in each variety and, consequently, provide further evidence on the potential influence of IndE in the Southeast Asian region. If clause-final also and only are used more frequently by younger speakers than by older speakers and/or more frequently by female speakers than by male speakers, we can infer that these features are becoming more frequent in these varieties.

Note what this means precisely: they are saying

  • “If clause-final also and only are used more frequently by younger speakers than by older speakers”: I understand that as talking about a main effect of AGE (since they are not combining the effect of younger vs. older with anything else such as SEX/GENDER or VARIETY);
  • “If clause-final also and only are used […] more frequently by female speakers than by male speakers”: I understand that as talking about a main effect of SEX/GENDER (since they are not combining the effect of female vs. male with anything else such as AGE or VARIETY).

(BTW, note that they combine these two propositions with “and/or”, which is pretty vague/permissive.) Then, as you saw, in their methods section on p. 7, they follow the above up with this:

Therefore, if the relative proportion of the use of innovative also and only by young (and) female speakers is higher than that of older (and) male speakers in both PhiE and HKE (when compared with IndE), this would indicate that the use of the feature is a more recent innovation in the former two varieties. This, in turn, would lend further support to the argument that IndE is an emerging epicentre in South(east) Asia.

To me, this says something statistically different because some of these statements clearly seem to involve interactions: They are saying “if the relative proportion of [innovative uses] by young (and) female speakers is higher than that of older (and) male speakers in both PhiE and HKE (when compared with IndE)”: This is tricky because of the difference to the above: This is an interaction prediction because they stipulate a difference of younger vs. older speakers “(and)” a difference of female vs. male speakers, but apparently only for certain possible comparisons depending on VARIETY, namely ‘when PhiE and HKE are compared to IndE’; that was our definition of an interaction: something (AGE ‘and/or’ GENDER) doesn’t do the same everywhere/always. Thus,

  • given the ambiguous permissiveness of “(and)”, are they predicting that young female speakers will behave differently from old male speakers? That young female speakers will behave differently from all old speakers (regardless of gender)? That young female speakers will behave differently from all male speakers (regardless of age)? That young speakers of both genders will have differently from old speakers of both genders? That female speakers of all ages will behave differently from male speakers of all ages? Some other combinations of (subsets of) things?
  • given the vaguenesss of the “in both PhiE and HKE (when compared with IndE)”, do they mean to compare any or all of the above comparisons like this: some subgroup of PhiE against the same subgroup of IndE and then separately some subgroup of HKE against the same subgroup of IndE? Or, remember orthogonal contrasts, for any or all of the above comparisons, will HKE and PhiE be grouped together against IndE?

I know you’re rolling your eyes at my obsessiveness, but you have to look at it this way: Hypothesis-testing with multifactorial regressions is all about the values of the coefficients (and of course their standard errors, etc. and p-values), but, as I said in the book, coefficients are (instructions for) comparisons. Now in P&F’s design, AGE has 4 levels, GENDER as 2, VARIETY has 3, and PARTICLE has 2. Thus, there are \(4 \times 2 \times 3 \times 2 = 48\) means that can be compared, which means there are \((48 \times 47) \div 2 = 1128\) comparisons that can theoretically be made (or \((24 \times 23) \div 2 = 276\) within each particle), and that purely theoretical maximum number does not yet even include the additional averages and comparisons that arise when you average across one or more of the variables (e.g. when you predict an absence of an interaction) or when you conflate variables. Even as you recoil at my obsessiveness, I hope you agree that hypotheses are not useful or instructive if they are phrased so vaguely that you don’t know which of the theoretically possible comparisons you’re interested in.

The final aspect of this disection of their exact formulation brings us to this: They are saying “if the relative proportion of the use of innovative also and only […]”: I understand this as not hypothesizing different effects/distributions for both particles, right? They’re not making any statement with regard to the particles differing in their behavior. Now, that brings us to the second set of book sections their paper should have triggered in your mind, namely Sections 5.2.4 and 5.2.8. I hope you realized that P&F made the same mistake as the presenter I discussed in Section 5.2.8: P&F fit two models – one for each particle – when there’s no real reason to not have fitted just one on all data and with PARTICLE as an additional predictor that can interact with every other predictor (like already implied above in the discussion of the \((48 \times 47) \div 2 = 1128\) comparisons). If their formulation above is correct in its implication that there will be no difference between the particles, then that will be seen in the absence of interactions of PARTICLE with any other predictor in their final model. If, on the other hand, the particles make a difference, then we’ll see that in interactions such as PARTICLE:AGE or PARTICLE:GENDER or PARTICLE:VARIETY or some 3-way interaction or even a 4-way interaction. Still and especially given the fact that they do not seem to anticipate particle-specific differences, there is not a single good reason to do two separate models rather than just one. (Re-read section 5.2.8 if you have to to understand this point, do not gloss over this!)

With regard to third and final triggering, the mixed-effects modeling one, the first fundamental problem is that both regressions are potentially extremely misleading. This is because each of the models is fit on values that are averages for each combination of all predictor levels, deleting all information regarding what individual speakers did. For instance, if you look at x[1,], …

x[1,]
##   CASE PARTICLE   AGE GENDER SPKRS VARIETY TOKENS  WORDS PMWpaper      PMW
## 1    1     also 14–25 female    84     HKE     45 107796      417 417.4552

… then you’ll see that for 14-25 year-old female speakers of HKE, P&F are reporting a frequency of 417 pmw of also (our own computation from their numbers yielded a value of 417.46) – but that one ‘clean’ value is an average of the values of x[1,"SPKRS"]=84 speakers (with unknown volatility). Similarly, if you look at x[26,], …

x[26,]
##    CASE PARTICLE   AGE GENDER SPKRS VARIETY TOKENS WORDS PMWpaper      PMW
## 26   26     only 26–35 female     3     HKE      2  5304      377 377.0739

… then you’ll see that for 26-35 year-old female speakers of HKE, P&F are reporting a frequency of 377 pmw of only – but that pretty similar value is based on a mere 3 speakers (with equally unknown volatility). Even worse, looking at x[17,]

x[17,]
##    CASE PARTICLE   AGE GENDER SPKRS VARIETY TOKENS WORDS PMWpaper      PMW
## 17   17     also 14–25 female   170    PhiE     37 93197      397 397.0085

shows that PMW, which is in the middle of the other two is the result of 170 speakers’ usage, but the way the analysis is performed, it misses all of that information. In other words, P&F are doing something akin to a by-items regression, which is precisely what mixed-effects models help avoid: “mixed-effects models do not require prior averaging” (Baayen, Davidson, & Bates 2008:399) of the by-subjects/by-items or, here, by-condition kind.

P&F’s modeling decision has multiple consequences, as a result of which their results cannot really be trusted: they might be right, they might be wrong – we cannot know. If you (or they) think this judgment is considered extreme and harsh, consider the following three points:

  1. Baayen et al. state “these procedures [by-item regression and by-participant regression] combine precise and imprecise information on an equal footing” and a regression based on averages for the combination of three predictors – AGE, GEnDER, and VARIETY – will be even more imprecise in how even more speaker-specific variability is swept under the table.
  2. One way to think about this imprecision is to recognize that we don’t know whether whether the 45 instances of also (from which P&F compute their 417 pmw frequency) for 14-25 year-old female speakers of HKE are
    1. from 1 speaker (with the 83 others never ever using also clause-finally), i.e. maximal repetition within one speaker;
    2. from 45 speakers each using it once (with the 39 others never using also clause-finally), i.e. no repetition within one speaker; or
    3. from anything in-between; …
    4. although it should be obvious that these three scenarios mean very different things. Mixed-effects modeling could change the results dramatically, depending on how the tokens are distributed across the speakers.
  3. This also means that P&F’s regression analysis doesn’t benefit from what we discussed in Chapter 6, namely shrinkage, i.e. the reduction of estimates for levels of random effects, which here could be SPEAKER, in a way that reflects the number of observations available for them and their variance or, as Baayen (2008:277) puts it, “[considers] the behavior of any given subject in the light of what it knows about the behavior of all the other subjects”.
