The present analysis takes the above values of 417 and 377 equally ‘seriously’, or it given them an equal weight in the analysis, because ‘it doesn’t know’ that the former is based on 84 speakers (with some variability) and the latter only on 3 (with some variability) – a mixed-effects model could be set up to shrink coefficients to adjust for the fact that the data contain a lot of evidence for the 417 and hardly any for the 377 and the variability summarized in the average, which is clearly desirable.

An additional, maybe smaller issue is concerned with something we saw above for quite. The data P&F provide in their appendix contain a few cases where PMW is 0:

x[x$PMW==0,]
##    CASE PARTICLE   AGE GENDER SPKRS VARIETY TOKENS WORDS PMWpaper PMW
## 2     2     also 26–35 female     3     HKE      0  5304        0   0
## 3     3     also 36–50 female     6     HKE      0  5888        0   0
## 4     4     also   >50 female     0     HKE      0     0        0   0
## 6     6     also 26–35   male     2     HKE      0  2892        0   0
## 7     7     also 36–50   male     4     HKE      0  3204        0   0
## 8     8     also   >50   male     1     HKE      0   902        0   0
## 24   24     also   >50   male     1    PhiE      0  3937        0   0
## 27   27     only 36–50 female     6     HKE      0  5888        0   0
## 28   28     only   >50 female     0     HKE      0     0        0   0
## 30   30     only 26–35   male     2     HKE      0  2892        0   0
## 31   31     only 36–50   male     4     HKE      0  3204        0   0
## 32   32     only   >50   male     1     HKE      0   902        0   0
## 36   36     only   >50 female     9    IndE      0  6757        0   0
## 44   44     only   >50 female     0    PhiE      0  3613        0   0
## 45   45     only 14–25   male    69    PhiE      0 32386        0   0
## 47   47     only 36–50   male   106    PhiE      0  6190        0   0
x[x$SPKRS==0,]
##    CASE PARTICLE AGE GENDER SPKRS VARIETY TOKENS WORDS PMWpaper      PMW
## 4     4     also >50 female     0     HKE      0     0        0   0.0000
## 20   20     also >50 female     0    PhiE      2  3613      554 553.5566
## 28   28     only >50 female     0     HKE      0     0        0   0.0000
## 44   44     only >50 female     0    PhiE      0  3613        0   0.0000

But remember: With quite in the learner data, 0s could mean different things: ‘a learner knows the word but just never decided to use it’ or ‘the learner doesn’t even know the word’. Here, the situation is that a value of 0 of PMW can mean ‘there are speakers but they didn’t use a clause-final particle’ or, as we have here, ‘no such speakers were attested in the data and that’s why we have no clause-final particles’. Here, P&F didn’t have any female speakers of HKE that were older than 50 (and maybe also no female speakers of PhiE that were older than 50, recall the note above). It’s not clear to me that one would want to leave those in the data: PMW values of 0 mean different things, depending on the SPKRS values of the same rows. I would, therefore, probably delete these two rows from the data set.

Finally and as an aside, let’s scrutinize their summary table of the results, which is repeated here for convenience and which I do not understand:

Clause-final Effect IndE HKE PhiE
also AGE: younger > older – (n.s.) –
also GENDER: female > male (p<0.01) (n.s.) (n.s.)
only AGE: younger > older (up to p<0.001) (n.s.) –
only GENDER: female > male – (n.s.) –

Their analysis of also ended with a model that did not have AGE in it so why does the top row of this table say “–” for IndE and PhiE but “n.s.” for HKE? If anything, shouldn’t it say “n.s.” for all three, given that AGE did not make it into the final model in that analysis? Similarly, P&F’s analysis of only ended with a model that did not have GENDER in it so why does the bottom row of this table say “–” for IndE and PhiE but “n.s.” for HKE? Just like with also, shouldn’t it say “n.s.” for all three, given that GENDER had no significant effect at all in that analysis?

But there’s more: Why does the 2nd row from the bottom say “n.s.” for HKE but “–” for PhiE? According to their post-hoc tests for only, repeated here for convenience …

pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.only, ~ AGE | VARIETY), adjust="Tukey")
## VARIETY = IndE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35   450.75 151 12   2.984  0.0487
##  14–25 - 36–50   589.31 151 12   3.901  0.0098
##  14–25 - >50     959.69 151 12   6.353  0.0002
##  26–35 - 36–50   138.57 151 12   0.917  0.7962
##  26–35 - >50     508.95 151 12   3.369  0.0249
##  36–50 - >50     370.38 151 12   2.452  0.1195
## 
## VARIETY = HKE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35   -23.90 151 12  -0.158  0.9985
##  14–25 - 36–50   164.64 151 12   1.090  0.7020
##  14–25 - >50     164.64 151 12   1.090  0.7020
##  26–35 - 36–50   188.54 151 12   1.248  0.6102
##  26–35 - >50     188.54 151 12   1.248  0.6102
##  36–50 - >50       0.00 151 12   0.000  1.0000
## 
## VARIETY = PhiE:
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35    15.52 151 12   0.103  0.9996
##  14–25 - 36–50    21.55 151 12   0.143  0.9989
##  14–25 - >50     -62.62 151 12  -0.415  0.9749
##  26–35 - 36–50     6.03 151 12   0.040  1.0000
##  26–35 - >50     -78.14 151 12  -0.517  0.9533
##  36–50 - >50     -84.17 151 12  -0.557  0.9428
## 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 4 estimates

… all comparisons between all levels of AGE in HKE and PhiE were not significant (all pTukey>0.61) so, if the hypotheses of trends for AGE are not confirmed for both HKE and PhiE, as they aren’t, why are results for the two varieties reported differently? To my mind, this is what this table should look like (with one caveat to be mentioned presently):

Clause-final Effect IndE HKE PhiE
also AGE: younger > older (n.s.) (n.s.) (n.s.)
also GENDER: female > male (p<0.01) (n.s.) (n.s.)
only AGE: younger > older (up to p<0.001) (n.s.) (n.s.)
only GENDER: female > male (n.s.) (n.s.) (n.s.)

Finally, what’s the statistical basis for, in this table, suddenly only conflating the 4 age groups into 2 (younger vs. older)? P&F don’t have a significance test for that because even the emmeans computation of all post-hoc tests only tested all 4 levels against each other – at no point was there a conflation of, say, the two younger and the two older age groups so what is this based on? (I did a separate model selection process for also with such a conflated version of AGE (not shown here), which did not make it into the final model either, but they are not reporting any such check, and, as you will see below, if anything it seems that the data are more compatible with a division of AGE into 3 levels rather than 4 or 2).

So, what should P&F have done instead? Obviously the hypotheses would have needed to be formulated much clearer, but what about the modeling improvements? We talked about some improvements already but let’s pull them all together: First, one important improvement to what they did would be to fit one model, not two, and that one model would have been fit on all the data, i.e. on also and only together, but with an additional predictor PARTICLE so that one would be able to see whether the effects of the other predictors – AGE, GENDER, and VARIETY – are the same for both particles or not, meaning, given that their model selection already allowed for a 3-way interaction, this model should have allowed for the possibility of a 4-way interaction. This is something we can actually do and will do below. Also, there are other small tweaks, one of which we already implemented, namely making AGE a predictor with successive-differences contrasts because that variable is ordinal, not categorical. Also, given the current vagueness of the hypotheses, it would make sense to use orthogonal contrasts on VARIETY such that comparison 1 would be IndE vs. HKE/PhiE combined, comparison 2 would be HKE vs. PhiE.

Second, a bigger change would be to change the response variable in such a way that speaker-specific variability can be taken into consideration (which we cannot do here, because we don’t have their data at the level of resolution this would require). I can think of two options here. One is to proceed as we did above for quite. The corresponding data frame would have sum(x$WORDS)=1093738, i.e. one for each word, the response variable would be a binary variable PRESENCE, stating whether each word is a clause-final particle: no vs. yes, and the predictors would be AGE, GENDER, VARIETY, and PARTICLE, and something like SPEAKER would be a random effect. This might then lead to a model like glmer(PRESENCE ~ AGE*GENDER*VARIETY*PARTICLE + (1+PARTICLE|SPEAKER), family=binomial, ...). In such a model,

  • the varying intercepts accommodate speakers’ different baselines to use either particle clause-finally;
  • no random slope AGE|SPEAKER because AGE wouldn’t vary within a speaker;
  • no random slope GENDER|SPEAKER because GENDER wouldn’t vary within a speaker;
  • no random slope VARIETY|SPEAKER because VARIETY wouldn’t vary within a speaker;
  • a random slope PARTICLE|SPEAKER because PARICLE can vary within a speaker.

The other option might theoretically be to have a data frame with as many rows as there are speakers (907 or 910, depending on what the correct number of male speakers of PhiE between 26 and 35 years of age is) times two (for each particle). The response variable would then be the relative frequency of the use of a particle by each speaker PMWperSPKR, and the predictors would be AGE, GENDER, VARIETY, and PARTICLE, and SPEAKER would again be a random effect. This might perhaps lead to a model like lmer(PMWperSPKR ~ AGE*GENDER*VARIETY*PARTICLE + (1|SPEAKER), ...) or a beta regression that’s good for responses that are proportions.

We of course cannot do either mixed-effects modeling analysis, because we only have the averaged-across-conditions data. But there is something else one could consider – I didn’t discuss it in the book so I didn’t expect you to come up with it straight away, but I will at least point you in some direction further below.

2.2.1 Improvement 1: the ‘big’ model

For our first round of improvements, we define contrasts that are compatible with P&F’s expectations regarding the varieties:

ind_vs_other <- c(2/3, -1/3, -1/3) # define contrast 1 w/ useful name
hke_vs_phi   <- c(0  ,  1/2, -1/2) # define contrast 2 w/ useful name
# & then we put those into matrix like the above table & assign them as contrasts:
(contrasts(x$VARIETY) <- # make the following the contrasts of VARIETY
   cbind(           # the matrix resulting from binding together as columns
      ind_vs_other, # the 1st contrast vector/column
      hke_vs_phi))  # the 2nd contrast vector/column
##      ind_vs_other hke_vs_phi
## [1,]    0.6666667        0.0
## [2,]   -0.3333333        0.5
## [3,]   -0.3333333       -0.5

Thus, in the modeling output below, pay attention to how these coefficients and their p-values help us understand things better already from the regression summary. As for AGE, the succ

Also, we already re-ordered the levels of the variable AGE but now we also use successive difference coding with it so we get coefficients for the age differences in ascending order and do more justice to the ordinal nature of the predictor:

contrasts(x$AGE) <- contr.sdif

Ok, so here we first attempt the first, smaller improvement, the big model on both particles with PARTICLE as a predictor (just to leave the data as comparable to them as possible, I’m leaving the two data points with SPKRS=0 in there; I also do a fully automatic model selection process again just for comparability):

summary(m.final.all <- stepAIC(
   lm(PMW ~ 1, data=x),
   scope=list(
      lower= ~1,
      upper= ~AGE*GENDER*VARIETY*PARTICLE),
   trace=0)) # do not show all the steps, set to 1 or omit for more details
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + PARTICLE + GENDER + AGE + VARIETY:PARTICLE, 
##     data = x)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -535.95 -145.81  -24.62   95.75  622.02 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)                        647.20      61.26  10.565 7.24e-13 ***
## VARIETYind_vs_other                996.28     106.10   9.390 1.91e-11 ***
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi                 -318.57     122.51  -2.600  0.01320 *  
## PARTICLEonly                      -338.40      70.73  -4.784 2.60e-05 ***
## GENDERmale                        -131.99      70.73  -1.866  0.06978 .  
## AGE2-1                            -179.04     100.03  -1.790  0.08145 .  
## AGE3-2                              47.12     100.03   0.471  0.64032    
## AGE4-3                            -104.30     100.03  -1.043  0.30368    
## VARIETYind_vs_other:PARTICLEonly  -506.43     150.05  -3.375  0.00171 ** 
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi:PARTICLEonly     336.09     173.26   1.940  0.05985 .  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 245 on 38 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.7965, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7483 
## F-statistic: 16.52 on 9 and 38 DF,  p-value: 1.305e-10
# Confint(m.final.all) # return estimates w/ their 95%-CI

Again pretty good R2s, although a bit below what we saw in the separate models. What does drop1 tell us?

drop1(          # drop each droppable predictor at a time
   m.final.all, # from the model m.final.all &
   test="F")    # test its significance w/ an F-test
## Single term deletions
## 
## Model:
## PMW ~ VARIETY + PARTICLE + GENDER + AGE + VARIETY:PARTICLE
##                  Df Sum of Sq     RSS    AIC F value   Pr(>F)   
## <none>                        2281463 536.92                    
## GENDER            1    209047 2490510 539.13  3.4819 0.069777 . 
## AGE               3    364895 2646358 538.04  2.0259 0.126581   
## VARIETY:PARTICLE  2    909832 3191296 549.03  7.5771 0.001701 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

This is ‘interesting’: AIC turns out to be quite permissive here: the usually stricter significance-based approach would not leave AGE and GENDER in. In other words, if step used F-tests, as they, I think incorrectly, wrote, the relevant apparent-time predictors wouldn’t even have survived the modeling – the only thing significant would have been the frequencies of particle uses across varieties regardless of AGE and GENDER!

But ok, for comparability’s sake we’re going with AIC, which means we have these 3 predictors to deal with – once we checked the diagnostics:

gvlma::gvlma(m.final.all) # fine
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + PARTICLE + GENDER + AGE + VARIETY:PARTICLE, 
##     data = x)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                      (Intercept)               VARIETYind_vs_other  
##                           647.20                            996.28  
##                VARIETYhke_vs_phi                      PARTICLEonly  
##                          -318.57                           -338.40  
##                       GENDERmale                            AGE2-1  
##                          -131.99                           -179.04  
##                           AGE3-2                            AGE4-3  
##                            47.12                           -104.30  
## VARIETYind_vs_other:PARTICLEonly    VARIETYhke_vs_phi:PARTICLEonly  
##                          -506.43                            336.09  
## 
## 
## ASSESSMENT OF THE LINEAR MODEL ASSUMPTIONS
## USING THE GLOBAL TEST ON 4 DEGREES-OF-FREEDOM:
## Level of Significance =  0.05 
## 
## Call:
##  gvlma::gvlma(x = m.final.all) 
## 
##                    Value p-value                Decision
## Global Stat        7.827 0.09812 Assumptions acceptable.
## Skewness           2.699 0.10038 Assumptions acceptable.
## Kurtosis           1.808 0.17875 Assumptions acceptable.
## Link Function      3.111 0.07778 Assumptions acceptable.
## Heteroscedasticity 0.209 0.64755 Assumptions acceptable.

2.2.1.1 The effect of GENDER

Let’s compute the predictions for the first main effect. We see that women use clause-final particles more (as one might have expected), but now, that we ran one model, not two, we also see that that effect does not differ across age groups, varieties, and particles – very nice.

(ge.df <- data.frame( # make ge.df a data frame of
   ge <- effect(      # an object called ge (for gender): the effect
      "GENDER",       # of GENDER
      m.final.all)))  # in m.final.all
##   GENDER      fit       se    lower    upper
## 1 female 478.0059 50.01604 376.7538 579.2581
## 2   male 346.0189 50.01604 244.7667 447.2711

Let’s visualize just for the sake of consistency:

plot(ge, ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

2.2.1.2 The effect of AGE

Let’s compute the predictions for the second main effect. If we bend over backwards real hard, we can ‘kinda’ see the maybe expected effect that, with increased age, the frequencies of clause-final particles go down, but this is really not particularly clear: The overall p-value of AGE was ≈0.13 and you can see in the summary of the model that the p-values for successive differences do not reach standard levels of significance. However (!), if you hypothesized a decreasing trend difference between each age group and the next, you might consider the difference between age group 1 (14-25) and age group 2 (26-35) significant, because half of p=0.08145 is in fact <0.05. That being said, all possible post-hoc comparisons, when corrected for multiple testing, are of course ns:

(ag.df <- data.frame( # make ag.df a data frame of
   ag <- effect(      # an object called ag (for age): the effect
      "AGE",          # of AGE
      m.final.all)))  # in m.final.all
##     AGE      fit       se    lower    upper
## 1 14–25 548.8100 70.73336 405.6178 692.0022
## 2 26–35 369.7697 70.73336 226.5775 512.9619
## 3 36–50 416.8867 70.73336 273.6945 560.0789
## 4   >50 312.5833 70.73336 169.3911 455.7755
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.all, ~ AGE), adjust="Holm") # of AGE
##  contrast      estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  14–25 - 26–35    179.0 100 38   1.790  0.4073
##  14–25 - 36–50    131.9 100 38   1.319  0.7805
##  14–25 - >50      236.2 100 38   2.362  0.1406
##  26–35 - 36–50    -47.1 100 38  -0.471  1.0000
##  26–35 - >50       57.2 100 38   0.572  1.0000
##  36–50 - >50      104.3 100 38   1.043  0.9110
## 
## Results are averaged over the levels of: VARIETY, PARTICLE, GENDER 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 6 tests

Let’s visualize just for the sake of consistency:

plot(ag, ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

Given the results of the separate models and this plot, one might explore whether conflating the middle two age groups would be better – I’ll leave that up to you but you should really do it, because

  • you’re a conscientious reviewer, right?
  • you’re an even more conscientious reader of my wonderful book, right??
  • the results are interesting: the final model with the middle two levels of AGE conflated is better because
    • it gets higher R2s values than our current model;
    • it scores a relative likelihood value of 3 compared to our current model;
    • it doesn’t contain the same predictors as our current model, meaning it sheds light on things not discussed in P&F.

So, what are you waiting for? >:)

2.2.1.3 The effect of VARIETY:PARTICLE

Here are the predictions for the interaction of VARIETY:PARTICLE …

vp.df <- data.frame(      # make vp.df a data frame of
   vp <- effect(          # an object called vp: the effect
      "VARIETY:PARTICLE", # of VARIETY:PARTICLE
      m.final.all))       # in m.final.all
vp # either you now look at vp ...
## 
##  VARIETY*PARTICLE effect
##        PARTICLE
## VARIETY       also      only
##    IndE 1245.39603 569.38098
##    HKE    89.83477  88.29315
##    PhiE  408.40109  70.76855
vp.df # or at the usual data frame vp.df
##   VARIETY PARTICLE        fit       se      lower     upper
## 1    IndE     also 1245.39603 86.63032 1070.02211 1420.7700
## 2     HKE     also   89.83477 86.63032  -85.53915  265.2087
## 3    PhiE     also  408.40109 86.63032  233.02716  583.7750
## 4    IndE     only  569.38098 86.63032  394.00705  744.7549
## 5     HKE     only   88.29315 86.63032  -87.08078  263.6671
## 6    PhiE     only   70.76855 86.63032 -104.60537  246.1425

… the corresponding effects plots and post-hoc tests:

plot(vp, x.var="PARTICLE", grid=TRUE)

# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.all
# of PARTICLE within each level of VARIETY, adjust using Holm
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.all, ~ PARTICLE | VARIETY), adjust="Holm")
## VARIETY = IndE:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  also - only   676.02 123 38   5.518  <.0001
## 
## VARIETY = HKE:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  also - only     1.54 123 38   0.013  0.9900
## 
## VARIETY = PhiE:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  also - only   337.63 123 38   2.756  0.0089
## 
## Results are averaged over the levels of: GENDER, AGE
plot(vp, x.var="VARIETY", grid=TRUE)

# compute pairwise comparisons of means for the model m.final.all
# of VARIETY within each level of PARTICLE, adjust using Holm
pairs(emmeans::emmeans(m.final.all, ~ VARIETY | PARTICLE), adjust="Holm")
## PARTICLE = also:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE    1155.6 123 38   9.432  <.0001
##  IndE - PhiE    837.0 123 38   6.832  <.0001
##  HKE - PhiE    -318.6 123 38  -2.600  0.0132
## 
## PARTICLE = only:
##  contrast    estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  IndE - HKE     481.1 123 38   3.927  0.0007
##  IndE - PhiE    498.6 123 38   4.070  0.0007
##  HKE - PhiE      17.5 123 38   0.143  0.8870
## 
## Results are averaged over the levels of: GENDER, AGE 
## P value adjustment: holm method for 3 tests

Clearly and as per the first set of tests/plot, in HKE the two particles do not differ, but in the other two varieties clause-final also is much more frequent than in clause-final only. As per the second set of tests/plot, also is used most in IndE, less in PhiE, and least in HKE whereas only is used most in IndE, and less in HKE and PhiE together.

In sum, we find a pretty much expected overall effect for GENDER, a weak expected overall tendency for AGE (suggesting 3, not 4 age groups), and we find that the hypothesized epicentral variety of IndE indeed uses the clause-final particles much more than the other two (and that PhiE speakers use also more than HKE speakers).

2.2.2 Improvement 2: the ‘big’ model with weights

The above model addresses the first fundamental issue of P&F’s analysis, the fact that one model should be fit rather than two, but what about the other one? As mentioned above, we don’t have access to the full data so we do not know what’s behind any particular PMW value

  • unless SPKRS is 1, in which case we know exactly how many instances that one speaker produced; and
  • unless PMW is 0 and SPKRS is not, in which case we know that all those speakers did not produce any of the clause-final particles. (However, recall from above that there were also cases of PMW being 0 because SPKRS is 0, meaning there were no speakers for that pocket of the data, namely AGE: >50, SEX: female, and VARIETY: HKE and maybe also VARIETY: PhiE.)

Thus, we cannot do a mixed-effects model and reap all the benefits from that. However, this is a case where another approach might help at least a bit, namely we can fit the one big model on both particles with PARTICLE as an additional predictor (as we did above), but then also use the values in SPKRS, which state how many speakers are behind the PMW-values, as weights in the regression in what is called weighted (least squares) regression. This method seems deceptively simple – we just use the argument weights that lm is ‘ready for’, but this is still not an uncomplicated method.

summary(m.final.all.weighted <- stepAIC(
   lm(PMW ~ 1, data=x, # a model of PMW as a function of an intercept
      weights=SPKRS),  # use the numbers of speakers as weights
   scope=list(
      lower= ~1,
      upper= ~AGE*GENDER*VARIETY*PARTICLE),
   trace=0)) # same result as if one used MASS::rlm w/ those weights
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = PMW ~ VARIETY + PARTICLE + AGE + GENDER + VARIETY:PARTICLE + 
##     PARTICLE:AGE + VARIETY:AGE + VARIETY:GENDER, data = x, weights = SPKRS)
## 
## Weighted Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2036.5  -353.2     0.0   426.4  2061.9 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)                        747.57     113.39   6.593 9.99e-07 ***
## VARIETYind_vs_other                960.87     185.33   5.185 2.95e-05 ***
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi                 -177.01     320.13  -0.553  0.58565    
## PARTICLEonly                      -508.53      79.78  -6.374 1.67e-06 ***
## AGE2-1                            -201.52     145.67  -1.383  0.17981    
## AGE3-2                             267.53     166.70   1.605  0.12217    
## AGE4-3                             -20.59     397.57  -0.052  0.95914    
## GENDERmale                        -151.01      76.22  -1.981  0.05965 .  
## VARIETYind_vs_other:PARTICLEonly  -244.22     138.08  -1.769  0.09021 .  
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi:PARTICLEonly     205.15     164.16   1.250  0.22398    
## PARTICLEonly:AGE2-1                 25.31     133.40   0.190  0.85121    
## PARTICLEonly:AGE3-2               -438.14     144.23  -3.038  0.00585 ** 
## PARTICLEonly:AGE4-3               -136.08     257.90  -0.528  0.60280    
## VARIETYind_vs_other:AGE2-1        -277.14     227.48  -1.218  0.23547    
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi:AGE2-1          -148.17     363.40  -0.408  0.68724    
## VARIETYind_vs_other:AGE3-2         -43.70     255.52  -0.171  0.86569    
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi:AGE3-2          -352.31     428.69  -0.822  0.41962    
## VARIETYind_vs_other:AGE4-3          39.36     578.90   0.068  0.94639    
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi:AGE4-3           372.14    1118.20   0.333  0.74229    
## VARIETYind_vs_other:GENDERmale    -209.16     147.48  -1.418  0.16953    
## VARIETYhke_vs_phi:GENDERmale      -118.48     201.80  -0.587  0.56285    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1084 on 23 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.9211, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8525 
## F-statistic: 13.43 on 20 and 23 DF,  p-value: 2.341e-08
drop1(                   # drop each droppable predictor at a time
   m.final.all.weighted, # from the model m.final.all.weighted &
   test="F")             # test its significance w/ an F-test
## Single term deletions
## 
## Model:
## PMW ~ VARIETY + PARTICLE + AGE + GENDER + VARIETY:PARTICLE + 
##     PARTICLE:AGE + VARIETY:AGE + VARIETY:GENDER
##                  Df Sum of Sq      RSS    AIC F value   Pr(>F)   
## <none>                        27013244 677.55                    
## VARIETY:PARTICLE  2   4042094 31055338 680.24  1.7208 0.201171   
## PARTICLE:AGE      3  18339908 45353152 696.42  5.2051 0.006852 **
## VARIETY:AGE       6  17436437 44449681 689.46  2.4743 0.053951 . 
## VARIETY:GENDER    2   5231252 32244496 682.05  2.2270 0.130585   
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
plot(allEffects(m.final.all.weighted), ylim=c(0, 1750), grid=TRUE)

I won’t discuss this here much because we don’t even have error-free data and this method is complex, but you can see that, while the final model here is more complex – everything interacts with VARIETY – that higher degree of complexity seems supported because even the adjusted R2 of the more complex model with weights (0.8525) is much better than the one from the simpler model that had no weights (0.7483). At the same time, the only significant effect in the final model is PARTICLE:AGE, nothing else would make the cut if one adopted model selection using a p-value of 0.05 as the cut-off … So, something more to learn, regressions with weights, exactly what you wanted!

2.3 Concluding remarks

In sum, even if the last few pages didn’t exactly sound like that, there is a lot to appreciate about P&F’s study: They make their data available, they are doing an over-/underuse study but unlike much other work, they are adopting a regression-based approach that involves interactions, they correct for multiple post-hoc tests, and they make nice use of the speakers’ meta data – we mustn’t forget those things. Nevertheless, there is still a sizable number of problems, several of which undermine the replicability of the study as reported as well as the overall results they obtain; to recap,

  • the data they provide come with several errors and their tabular summaries of the results table are, minimally, inconsistent;
  • they do two regression analyses instead of one and thus do not compare their findings well across particles; also, it seems they misunderstood or misreported their model selection criterion;
  • rather than doing a mixed-effects model or at least a weighted regression, they do their regressions on averaged-by-condition data, which
    • homogenizes the data (by hiding the variance of the values from which the average is computed);
    • gives equal weight to extremely unequal speaker numbers;
  • they provide visualizations and interpretations that represent observed effects their final models do not actually contain.

In a review, I would also bring up the following less severe/categorical issues:

  • even for the models they do, I would want to see more numerical results: R2 and coefficients etc. for the effects they discuss – did you notice that we didn’t get p-values (or AIC-comparisons) for interactions, no summary tables, …?
  • even for the models they do, I would want to see predicted values (not observed ones) and indications of uncertainty for those (e.g., CIs).

Correcting at least the major issues – in particular fitting the big mixed-effects model with these data – could change the results considerably, as we saw in our smaller corrections already. Thus, at this point, as a reviewer, I would only recommend ‘revise/resubmit’ (while expecting that this could be accepted once the new analysis is in place); obviously, an editor might disagree, even more obviously, one editor didn’t send this out to a statistically knowledgeable reviewer and has, accordingly, already disagreed.

3 Optional “that”-complementation in native and learner English

Ok, this exercise is a big one, really big – at least if you really try hard. You will study the realization of the optional complementizer that in object and subject complementation by NS of English and NNS of English (with German and Spanish as their L1s). An example would be Seriously, I really hope very much Ø/that the Vorlons will help us against the Shadows, i.e. the response variable is COMPLEMENTIZER with two levels absent and present. For this study, the following variables are considered correlated with the choice to (not) realize that in such cases:

  • REGISTER: was the example spoken or written?
  • TYPE: the complementation type: object or subject, i.e. here object;
  • L_MATRBE4S: the length of any material before the matrix clause (in characters), i.e. here 9 (Seriously);
  • L_MATRSUBJ: the length of the matrix clause subject (in characters), i.e. here 1 (I);
  • L_MATRS2V: the length of any material between the subject and the verb of the matrix clause, i.e. here 6 (really);
  • L_MATRV2CC: the length of any material between the verb of the matrix clause and the complement clause, i.e. here 9 (very much);
  • L_COMP: the length of the complement clause (in characters), i.e. here 44 (the Vorlons will help us against the Shadows);
  • L_COMPSUBJ: the length of the complement clause subject (in characters), i.e. here 11 (the Vorlons);
  • L_COMPREST: the length of the rest of complement clause (in characters), i.e. here 32 (will help us against the Shadows).

The above variables are, so to speak, background/control variables that you should be controlling for as you’re targeting the following ‘new predictors’ whose effects you’re interested in:

  1. By now much work starting with Stefanowitsch & Gries (2003) and, for alternations, Gries & Stefanowitsch (2004) has shown that lexical items have sometimes very strong constructional preferences. Such preferences have been involved in priming research, but now also in complementation research, see, e.g. Jaeger (2010), who, however, operationalizes this as surprisal, which we will use separately here. To see whether there are such verb-specific effects in the present data set, you will discuss the following two predictors based on the measure called ΔP (see Ellis 2007 or Gries 2013):
    • DPWC: the degree to which the matrix clause verb ‘prefers’ that omission measured with the directional (!) association measure ΔPword2construction;
    • DPCW: the degree to which that omission prefers the specific matrix clause verb ‘prefers’ measured with the directional (!) association measure ΔPconstruction2word;
  2. Since there is also a growing body of work documenting predictability/surprisal effects in sentence processing (see, e.g., Linzen & Jaeger 2014, Lester 2018), the data include the variable of SURPRISAL, here the degree to which the last word of the matrix clause (above much) makes the first word of the complement clause (above the first the) surprising measured with the (usual) negative binary log of a weighted conditional probability p(the|much) from the British National Corpus.

In addition, in this learner corpus context, you will of course also be interested in the variable L1, which has the levels English, German, and Spanish. In terms of potential random effects, you could consider:

  • FILE: a proxy for the speaker who produced the example;
  • MATCHLEMMA: the matrix clause verb lemma, i.e. here hope.

The data are a subset of the data analyzed (differently) in Wulff, Gries, & Lester (2018). They are from the British Component of the International Corpus of English (ICE-GB) for the NS and from the International Corpus of Learner English (ICLE, Granger et al. 2009) and the Louvain International Database of Spoken English Interlanguage (LINDSEI, Gilquin et al. 2010), for the NNS:

summary(x <- read.table(            # show the summary of loading into x
   "../_inputfiles/06_thatcomplementation-ex.csv", # this tab-delimited file, which
   header=TRUE,                     # has a header in row 1
   sep="\t",                        # has tab-delimited columns
   quote="",                        # no quote are used
   comment.char="",                 # no comments are used
   stringsAsFactors=TRUE))          # make columns with char. strings factors
##       CASE          CORPUS          FILE        MATCHLEMMA   COMPLEMENTIZER  TYPE     
##  Min.   :   1   ICE-GB :3669   GE042  :  58   think  :2683   absent :3769   obj:5524  
##  1st Qu.:1544   ICLE   :1260   SP024  :  48   say    : 895   present:2405   sub: 650  
##  Median :3088   LINDSEI:1245   GE009  :  42   be     : 586                            
##  Mean   :3088                  S1A-022:  39   mean   : 428                            
##  3rd Qu.:4631                  S1B-039:  35   know   : 359                            
##  Max.   :6174                  S1A-054:  32   hope   : 153                            
##                                (Other):5920   (Other):1070                            
##        L1          REGISTER        L_COMP         L_COMPSUBJ        L_COMPREST    
##  English:3669   spoken :4206   Min.   :  4.00   Min.   :  1.000   Min.   :  1.00  
##  German :1369   written:1968   1st Qu.: 27.25   1st Qu.:  2.000   1st Qu.: 22.00  
##  Spanish:1136                  Median : 48.00   Median :  3.000   Median : 39.00  
##                                Mean   : 63.66   Mean   :  8.479   Mean   : 55.18  
##                                3rd Qu.: 82.00   3rd Qu.:  8.000   3rd Qu.: 72.00  
##                                Max.   :500.00   Max.   :189.000   Max.   :463.00  
##                                                                                   
##    L_MATRV2CC         L_MATRBE4S        L_MATRSUBJ        L_MATRS2V             DPCW          
##  Min.   : 0.00000   Min.   :  0.000   Min.   :  1.000   Min.   :  0.0000   Min.   :-0.113264  
##  1st Qu.: 0.00000   1st Qu.:  0.000   1st Qu.:  1.000   1st Qu.:  0.0000   1st Qu.:-0.003243  
##  Median : 0.00000   Median :  0.000   Median :  1.000   Median :  0.0000   Median : 0.035430  
##  Mean   : 0.04244   Mean   :  2.864   Mean   :  5.795   Mean   :  0.5011   Mean   : 0.153483  
##  3rd Qu.: 0.00000   3rd Qu.:  0.000   3rd Qu.:  4.000   3rd Qu.:  0.0000   3rd Qu.: 0.374783  
##  Max.   :69.00000   Max.   :202.000   Max.   :175.000   Max.   :119.0000   Max.   : 0.374783  
##                                                                                               
##       DPWC             SURPRISAL     
##  Min.   :-0.521334   Min.   : 1.343  
##  1st Qu.:-0.007668   1st Qu.: 4.094  
##  Median : 0.154076   Median : 5.045  
##  Mean   : 0.127165   Mean   : 6.100  
##  3rd Qu.: 0.421268   3rd Qu.: 7.020  
##  Max.   : 0.480777   Max.   :20.007  
##

Given the orientation of the case study, here is some information about how I ended up analyzing these data. This information is just to get you thinking; you might in fact end up analyzing the data differently. However, one thing is absolutely obligatory: You must do (i) an exhaustive univariate exploration of all variables involved – predictors, controls, and the response COMPLEMENTIZER – as well as (ii) some bivariate exploration of the relations between each predictor or control with COMPLEMENTIZER; let me already tell you that you will need to approach these data with an open mind when it comes to transforming predictors etc..

With regard to the modeling, I myself ended up with

  • a modeling process that started from one version of a ‘maximal model’;
  • some version of model selection to arrive at a final model:
    • in terms of selection order, I followed the strategy outlined by Zuur et al. (2009: Ch. 5) and (i) determined the ‘best’ ranef structure and then (ii) identified the ‘best’ fixef structure;
    • in terms of variable selection, I only considered the targeted/relevant predictors for deletion/removal, not the control variables;
    • in terms of selection criterion, I used likelihood ratio tests using a ‘traditional’ threshold of p=0.05 for the ranefs and a threshold of p=0.1 for the fixefs;
  • doing some basic model diagnostics during the modeling selection process, namely
    • collinearity (mostly in the form of variance inflation factors (VIFs));
    • overdispersion;
  • after the model selection process outlined above has been completed, I did proper final model diagnostics/model criticism;
  • and ended with model interpretation:
    • overall model assessment R2s, classification accuracy, precision, recall, and the C-score (AUC);
    • fixef results: coefficients, their CIs, and esp. their visualization by means of effects plots of model predictions;
    • ranef results exemplification: their effect on the fixefs.

For the fixef structure of my kind of ‘maximal model’,

  • the general predictors of interest are DPCW, DPWC, and SURPRISAL.wind.bcn and of course L1;
  • I used a variety of controls:
    • REGISTER and TYPE because these are known to correlate with our response variable;
    • a variety of controls that operationalize various aspects of complexity/processing load associated with the matrix clause: L_MATRBE4S.fac, L_MATRSUBJ.ord, and L_MATRS2V.fac;
    • a variety of controls that operationalize various aspects of complexity/processing load associated with the complement clause: L_COMPLCLPC and L_COMPSUBJ.bcn.

As for the ranef structure, I tried to follow Barr et al. (2013) as much as possible, full well knowing, however, that – as discussed esp. by Matuschek et al. 2017 and Bates et al. 2018 – that maximal ranef structure was likely to be in need of trimming down.

Ok, good luck with this. Now, as for the answer key, you have to know that this case study is in fact the topic of a Methods Showcase article on mixed-effects modeling for the journal Language Learning. The article itself is an introduction to mixed-effects modeling, which you really don’t need to read because you’ve read Chapter 6 of this book, which covers that topic in mode detail than I could cover things in the journal article. However, that article comes with a roughly 200-page online supplement, which is essentially the complete analysis, from loading the data and the initial exploration to the final plots/diagnostics, i.e. your answer key. So, give this a shot and then you can look up the answer key either on this OSF website or, if I end up updating that at some point, from my website here. Be advised though, this is a massive exercise and an incredibly recalcitrant observational data set, doing this analysis for real might take dozens of hours, so, if you really want to learn from this, you have to take it seriously – you won’t be able to take home as much from this exercise as you can if you try to do the whole thing during 2 hours between two meetings. So, don’t phone it in!

  1. Sometimes, such data can be dealt with better with so-called zero-inflated negative binomial models (see, e.g., the GLMMadaptive::mixed_model).↩︎
